title: Alan Wake 2
guide: Alan Wake 2 is Remedy Entertainment's long-awaited sequel to the 2010 cult horror title, switching between FBI Agent Saga Anderson investigating a ritual murder in Bright Falls and novelist Alan Wake trapped in the Dark Place. The walkthrough captures the full game across two parts on PS5 Pro at 4K HDR 60FPS with PSSR upscaling, no commentary. Remedy's signature live-action FMV cutscenes intercut with the cinematic gameplay.

chapters.name: 1. Return — The Cauldron Lake Murder
chapters.summary:
The campaign opens with **FBI Special Agent Saga Anderson** arriving in **Bright Falls, Washington** alongside her partner **Alex Casey** to investigate a ritualistic murder near Cauldron Lake. The opening hour is structured as a procedural police investigation — interview transcripts, crime-scene photography, autopsy reports — with the supernatural staying firmly off-camera until the chapter's mid-point. The Bright Falls of *Alan Wake II* is rendered as a town visibly worse off than the original game's: economically depressed, drug-touched, and quietly haunted by the lake at its center.

The chapter's central reveal is the body itself. The victim is found ritualistically arranged with pages from an unwritten manuscript stitched into his torso, and the autopsy reveals that the victim's heart has been removed and replaced with a different one — the heart of a man missing for thirteen years. The man is **Robert Nightingale**, the FBI agent from the original *Alan Wake* who disappeared into Cauldron Lake at that game's conclusion. The case immediately resists rational explanation.

The chapter introduces the campaign's **Mind Place** mechanic — Saga's psychological investigation space, where she pins photographs, interviews, and evidence to a cork-board and runs deductive logic in deliberate, slow-motion sequences. The Mind Place is the campaign's most acclaimed UI design and one of its most thematically loaded systems: Saga's mind is, in the campaign's own metaphysics, a location that other things can enter.

The chapter ends with Saga and Casey checking into the Bright Falls motel for an investigation expected to last three days. It will not last three days.

chapters.name: 2. Late Night — The Dark Place
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first **Alan Wake** chapter. Alan, missing for thirteen years since the conclusion of the original game, is revealed to be alive — barely — inside the **Dark Place**, an extradimensional prison made of nightmare-logic versions of New York City. The chapter opens with Alan emerging from black water in a subway station, with no memory of how long he has been there, and the Dark Place rendered as the campaign's most aesthetically distinctive environment: a Lynchian Manhattan analog where streetlights stutter, posters change between glances, and the city's architecture twists at impossible angles.

The chapter's central mechanic introduction is the **Plot Board** — Alan's narrative-modification system, in which he writes scenes onto a story-corkboard and the Dark Place itself rewrites to accommodate the new draft. The mechanic is one of the most original puzzle systems in modern gaming: the player must rewrite the locations of items, the identities of NPCs, and the meanings of objects in order to navigate the Dark Place's geography.

The chapter's atmospheric anchor is the **Cult of the Word** — a hooded faction of Dark Place residents who worship Alan as the prophet of their own escape. Their leader, the **Mr. Door**-affiliated figure who recurs throughout the campaign, is introduced through one of the most unsettling cult-recruitment sequences in modern horror gaming. The chapter ends with Alan accepting that he is going to have to write his way out, and that the writing itself is going to summon worse things into Bright Falls.

chapters.name: 3. Local Girl — The Watery Investigation
chapters.summary:
Saga's investigation extends north to **Watery**, a sub-region of the Bright Falls jurisdiction where the murdered man — Robert Nightingale — was last seen alive thirteen years ago. The chapter is structured as a slow-burn investigation across the lakeside town, with witness interviews, evidence collection, and one of the campaign's most uncomfortable interrogation sequences against a possessed local resident.

The chapter's central thread is the introduction of the **Anderson Brothers** — **Tor and Odin Anderson**, the aged frontmen of the fictional rock band Old Gods of Asgard whose music has been threaded through the franchise since the original game. The brothers are now elderly, living in a sprawling rural farm property, and visibly tangled with the supernatural in ways even they don't fully understand. Their farmhouse becomes one of the campaign's recurring locations.

The chapter ends with Saga uncovering a connection between Nightingale's murder, the Anderson Brothers' old recordings, and a manuscript fragment that appears to predict the murders before they happen. The supernatural is no longer deniable. The case is no longer procedural.

chapters.name: 4. Initiation — The Cult of the Word
chapters.summary:
Alan's second sustained chapter inside the Dark Place. The chapter is structured as a slow-creeping infiltration of the **Cult of the Word's** central hideout — a Lynchian, fluorescent-lit warehouse complex where the cult's members chant the manuscripts Alan has involuntarily written across the decade. The cult's worship is rendered with the kind of skin-crawling specificity that places this chapter among the most acclaimed horror sequences in modern gaming.

The chapter's central reveal is **Scratch** — Alan's doppelganger, an entity that has been writing alongside him from the Dark Place and is now manifesting in the real world. Scratch is presented as Alan's shadow self, a violent, predatory version of the man Alan was before he stopped writing thrillers. The campaign frames the Scratch threat as functionally an evil twin: indistinguishable from Alan in photographs, indistinguishable from Alan to his own wife, and committed to destroying Alan's path home.

The chapter's gameplay loop deepens the **Plot Board** mechanic with sustained narrative-rewrite puzzles, including the campaign's first sustained chase sequence against the Dark Place's primary predators. The chapter ends with Alan committing to a manuscript that will free him — and the realization that completing it will require Saga's cooperation across the dimensional barrier.

chapters.name: 5. The Heart — Saga's Mind Place Crisis
chapters.summary:
The campaign's mid-game turning point. Saga's investigation has uncovered enough evidence to enter a Mind Place vision of the Cauldron Lake murder itself, and the chapter is structured as a sustained sequence inside Saga's own deductive imagination. The Mind Place opens out into a multi-room interior — case files, family photographs, childhood memories — and the chapter dwells on Saga's history in ways the procedural opening hours refused to.

The chapter's central reveal is that Saga has been **manipulated by the Dark Place** from the beginning of the case. Her invitation to Bright Falls, her partnership with Casey, and her family's connection to the Anderson Brothers are all elements of a story Alan has been writing from inside the Dark Place — sometimes consciously, sometimes not. The chapter recontextualizes the entire campaign as a question of authorship: who is writing whom, and whether anyone in the Bright Falls case has actual agency.

The chapter ends with Saga's discovery of a hidden Mind Place chamber containing photographs of her own daughter — who in some versions of the story is dead, in others alive, in others never existed. The metaphysics of the Dark Place have begun to bleed into the real world.

chapters.name: 6. Old Gods of Asgard — Herald of Darkness
chapters.summary:
The campaign's most acclaimed single sequence and one of the most ambitious set-pieces in modern gaming. Saga's investigation leads her to the **Old Gods of Asgard concert hall**, a derelict Coca-Cola Hall venue beneath the Anderson Brothers' farm, where the band's classic-rock catalog has been weaponized into a literal reality-rewriting ritual. The chapter is structured as a single sustained performance of the song *Herald of Darkness*, with the Anderson Brothers re-recording themselves onstage in real-time while the chapter's combat and platforming sequences unfold in front of the audience.

The chapter is half music video, half rhythm-action set-piece, half combat encounter. The Anderson Brothers narrate Alan's entire backstory in song while the player navigates the venue's stage, lighting rigs, and audience pit against Taken-class enemies. The sequence has been cited as one of the most distinctive in PlayStation gaming history.

The chapter ends with Saga learning the full scope of the campaign's antagonist — not Scratch, not the Cult, but the entity that has been using Alan's writing as a vector for entering the real world. The chapter's title track plays out over a slow camera pull through the now-empty concert hall.

chapters.name: 7. Zane's Film — The Live-Action Hour
chapters.summary:
A chapter that briefly reshapes the entire campaign's medium. Alan, deep in the Dark Place's narrative-rewrite territory, accesses a sustained sequence inside **Thomas Zane**'s film archives — Zane being the metafictional director from the original *Alan Wake* who is now revealed to be a central figure in the Remedy Connected Universe's mythology. The chapter intercuts gameplay with **roughly an hour of live-action film footage** featuring real actors, real sets, and a sustained narrative segment that plays as a self-contained short film.

The chapter is the campaign's most experimental and one of its most divisive sequences. The live-action footage stages Zane's perspective on the events of the Dark Place, the role of the **Mr. Door**-affiliated antagonist, and the broader Remedy multiverse including *Control*'s Federal Bureau of Control. The chapter functions as both a deep-lore download and a tonal disruption that the campaign's final third pays off.

The chapter ends with Alan in possession of the film's central macguffin — a manuscript fragment that, when combined with Saga's evidence in the real world, will collapse the dimensional barrier and force the campaign's final confrontation.

chapters.name: 8. Deerfest — The Bright Falls Festival
chapters.summary:
Saga's chapter dedicated to the **Deerfest**, Bright Falls's annual harvest festival, which the campaign has been pacing toward since the opening hour. The chapter is staged across the festival grounds at dusk and into night, with carnival rides, drunken locals, and a slow-creeping sense that everyone in attendance is either possessed, hallucinating, or actively being rewritten by the Dark Place's encroachment.

The chapter's central event is the **possession of half the festival audience** by Taken-class entities, with Saga forced to navigate combat sequences against people she has interviewed throughout the case. The chapter's emotional weight comes from the impossibility of saving most of them — the campaign refuses heroic restoration, and the festival ends with significant casualties.

The chapter ends with Saga finally locating the Cauldron Lake entry point that Alan has been navigating from the other side, and the campaign's two protagonists confirmed to be on the verge of meeting for the first time in thirteen years.

chapters.name: 9. Come Home — The Final Manuscript
chapters.summary:
The campaign's climactic chapter. Alan and Saga, working in coordinated rewrites across the dimensional barrier, attempt to collapse the Dark Place's grip on Bright Falls before **Scratch** can complete his own takeover. The chapter is structured as a parallel-action finale, with Alan navigating the Dark Place's deepest layers and Saga defending the Cauldron Lake breach in the real world.

The chapter's mid-section is the campaign's most acclaimed boss confrontation: a multi-stage duel against Scratch that demands mastery of every system both characters have taught — Alan's narrative rewrites, Saga's case-board deductions, the campaign's combat fundamentals, and the Plot Board's environmental manipulation. The fight is staged across both dimensions simultaneously, with cuts between Alan and Saga's perspectives happening in mid-combat.

The chapter closes with the manuscript completed, Scratch defeated, and the campaign's final cliffhanger: **Alan does not return to the real world**. The barrier closes with him still inside the Dark Place, having sacrificed his own escape to ensure Saga's. The campaign's epilogue — staged in a half-lit hospital room — sets up *The Lake House* and *Night Springs* expansions while leaving the franchise's central question unresolved. The cycle is not yet over.

title: Banishers: Ghosts of New Eden
guide: Banishers: Ghosts of New Eden is Don't Nod's action RPG set in 17th century colonial New England. Red and Antea are Banishers — supernatural hunters tasked with exorcising the dead. After a tragic incident at the haunted village of New Eden, Antea becomes a ghost herself, and Red must choose between resurrecting her at terrible cost or letting her ascend. The walkthrough captures the full campaign across two parts on PS5 Pro at 4K HDR 60FPS with PSSR upscaling, no commentary.

chapters.name: 1. Arrival in New Eden
chapters.summary:
The campaign opens in **1695, in a remote New England coastal settlement called New Eden**. The protagonists — **Antea Duarte**, a Cuban-born Banisher, and her partner **Red mac Raith**, a Scottish-born Banisher — arrive at the town having been summoned by its desperate community. Banishers are professional ghost-hunters whose work consists of investigating supernatural incidents, identifying the spirits causing them, and either resolving the spirits' unfinished business (**Ascending** them) or destroying them outright (**Banishing** them).

The opening sequence is structured as a sustained character-introduction chapter. Antea and Red's professional partnership, romantic relationship, and shared philosophy regarding the supernatural are established through sustained dialogue exchanges across the chapter's opening hours. The chapter's first sustained combat sequence introduces the campaign's reworked combat fundamentals — Red's flintlock-and-sword melee combat alongside Antea's spectral interventions, with the player rapidly switching between the two protagonists in real-time.

The chapter ends with Antea and Red reaching New Eden's central settlement, the local pastor's brief on the community's haunting situation delivered, and the campaign's central investigation formally committed.

chapters.name: 2. The Nightmare — Antea's Death
chapters.summary:
The campaign's inciting tragedy. The local haunting is revealed as significantly more powerful than Antea and Red anticipated. **The Nightmare**, a senior-tier supernatural entity that has been corrupting the broader region's spiritual ecosystem, ambushes the Banishers at the settlement's church. The chapter is structured as a sustained combat sequence in collapsing church architecture, with Antea attempting to shield Red from the entity's final attack.

The chapter's pivot is **Antea's death**. The Nightmare's killing blow lands at the end of the sequence, with Antea dying in Red's arms. The campaign's central conceit emerges as Antea's spirit manifests as a permanent companion-ghost, bound to Red and to New Eden's region until either of two resolutions can be achieved.

The chapter ends with Red and Antea's mutual recognition of their new circumstance, the campaign's central moral framework introduced (**Ascension** or **Resurrection** as the two paths forward), and the broader investigation across New Eden's settlements committed.

chapters.name: 3. The Promise — Two Paths
chapters.summary:
The campaign's central moral pivot chapter. **Antea**, now a spectral companion, offers Red two paths: **Ascension** (her formal Banisher departure to the afterlife, requiring the resolution of the supernatural conflicts plaguing New Eden) or **Resurrection** (her return to physical life, requiring the deaths of those innocent townspeople responsible for unresolved supernatural incidents in the region). The chapter is structured as a sustained dialogue sequence at the Banishers' encampment.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's central choice architecture. Each subsequent case investigation across New Eden's settlements will present Red with a moral decision — does he resolve the supernatural conflict by Ascending the responsible spirit, by Banishing it, or by **blaming a living person** for the haunting (thereby moving Antea closer to Resurrection)? The choices accumulate across the campaign's runtime and determine its multiple endings.

The chapter ends with Red committing to investigate New Eden's outstanding cases, the campaign's open-world exploration loop fully active, and the franchise's signature investigation-and-judgment gameplay loop committed.

chapters.name: 4. The First Settlements — Wakefield and Salem Springs
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first sustained investigation chapter. Red and Antea visit the smaller satellite settlements around New Eden — **Wakefield**, a struggling colonial village; **Salem Springs**, a religious enclave with extreme practices — to investigate haunting reports. The chapter is structured as a sustained open-world exploration sequence weaving combat against supernatural entities with the franchise's signature case-investigation gameplay.

The chapter introduces the campaign's signature **Haunting Cases** system. Each case involves the player gathering testimony from multiple townspeople, examining physical evidence at the haunting site, identifying the spirit causing the disturbance, and making the moral choice that determines the case's resolution. The cases are interconnected — choices in earlier cases affect the testimony available in later ones.

The chapter ends with the first several Haunting Cases resolved according to the player's chosen path, the campaign's emerging moral pattern (Red's gradual commitment toward either Ascension or Resurrection) becoming visible, and the path to deeper investigations opened.

chapters.name: 5. The Curse of New Eden
chapters.summary:
The campaign's central investigation chapter. Red and Antea's investigations reveal that **a regional curse** has been operating across New Eden for decades, with the haunting incidents the Banishers have been investigating as individual cases all tied to a single broader supernatural infestation. The chapter dwells on the franchise's environmental storytelling, with the curse's origins traced through colonial-era documents, surviving Indigenous testimony from the region's Native peoples, and the slow recognition that the original European settlers brought the curse with them.

The chapter introduces the **Indigenous Banisher** subplot. The region's Native communities have their own spiritual traditions that include something resembling Banishing, and Red's investigation forces him to navigate cultural negotiations the colonial Banisher tradition has not previously prepared him for. The chapter's most thematically loaded sequences dwell on the campaign's central postcolonial tensions.

The chapter ends with the curse's central source identified, the path to its resolution committed, and the campaign's third-act setup positioned.

chapters.name: 6. The Hunt for the Source — The Old Forest
chapters.summary:
The campaign's mid-third-act setting. The curse's central source is identified as a corrupted spiritual nexus deep within the **Old Forest** that surrounds New Eden. The chapter is structured as a sustained exploration sequence through the forest's increasingly distorted environments, with the campaign's most demanding sustained combat encounters and the franchise's most acclaimed environmental design.

The chapter weaves combat with sustained environmental investigation. The forest is rendered with significantly more visual detail than the campaign's settlement environments — twisting trees, supernatural fog, and the campaign's most distinctive aesthetic. The chapter ends with the Old Forest's central nexus located, the curse's broader infrastructure mapped, and the path to the campaign's climactic resolution opened.

chapters.name: 7. The Final Choice — Ascension or Resurrection
chapters.summary:
The campaign's climactic chapter. Red and Antea reach the central source of the regional curse, with the chapter staged as a sustained boss-confrontation sequence followed by the campaign's final moral decision. The chapter dwells on the campaign's central choice architecture — Red's accumulated investigation choices across the prior chapters have positioned Antea toward either Ascension (the morally clean path) or Resurrection (the morally compromised path).

The chapter's boss confrontation is staged across the curse's central nexus, with the campaign's most demanding sustained combat sequence. The fight's pacing depends on the player's accumulated Banisher specialization — combat-focused or investigation-focused builds produce different boss confrontation difficulty curves.

The chapter ends with Red's final commitment to one of three possible endings: **Ascension** (Antea formally departs to the afterlife, the curse is fully resolved, the colonial New Eden is set on a path toward genuine peace), **Resurrection** (Antea returns to physical life, the curse is incompletely resolved, and the campaign's moral framework is permanently fractured), or **Failure** (Antea is permanently lost, with the campaign's epilogue staged as Red's quiet defeat).

chapters.name: 8. Epilogue — The Final Settlement
chapters.summary:
The campaign's epilogue chapter is staged in the days following the central confrontation. Red's accumulated choices determine the epilogue's specific configuration — which settlements survived, which families were judged, which broader supernatural ecosystem has been restored. The chapter is structured as a quiet exploration sequence with no combat, dedicated entirely to the campaign's emotional resolutions.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's thematic resolutions. The Banisher tradition's central ethical framework — that supernatural intervention requires the practitioner to make moral judgments that cannot be unmade — is foregrounded across the epilogue's final dialogue exchanges. The chapter ends with Red's quiet departure from New Eden (alone or alongside resurrected Antea, depending on the player's chosen ending), the credits rolling over the surviving settlement's slow restoration to ordinary life.

The campaign's central question — whether the Banisher tradition's moral burden can be honored in a colonial context that has corrupted its practitioners — is answered through the player's accumulated choices rather than through any single ending sequence.

title: Control Ultimate Edition
guide: Control Ultimate Edition is Remedy Entertainment's supernatural action-adventure inside The Oldest House, headquarters of the Federal Bureau of Control. Jesse Faden arrives searching for her missing brother and unwittingly becomes the Bureau's new director after the previous director's suicide. The walkthrough captures the full main campaign plus AWE and Foundation DLC across two parts on PS5 Pro at 4K HDR 60FPS with PSSR upscaling, no commentary.

chapters.name: 1. Welcome to the Oldest House
chapters.summary:
The campaign opens with **Jesse Faden**, a twenty-something woman who has spent seventeen years searching for her missing brother **Dylan**, arriving at the **Oldest House** — the headquarters of the Federal Bureau of Control in a fictionalized New York City. The opening sequence is structured as a slow-burn exploration of the building's Brutalist interior, with Jesse navigating the empty corridors, the desk of an apparently suicide-stricken director, and the **Service Weapon** — a shape-shifting parautilitarian firearm that is the Bureau's signature artifact.

The chapter introduces the campaign's central conceit: the Oldest House is not a normal building. It is a **Place of Power**, a paranatural structure with shifting geography, telekinetic anomalies, and an extradimensional entity called the **Board** that communicates through reverberant whispers. The Service Weapon binds to Jesse — an act that, by Bureau tradition, makes her the new Director by inheritance. The chapter ends with Jesse formally accepting the role, her connection to Dylan's whereabouts now staged through Bureau records.

chapters.name: 2. Unknown Caller
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first sustained combat chapter. **The Hiss** — a paranatural entity that has infected most of the Bureau's staff and is broadcasting a corrupting frequency throughout the Oldest House — manifests as the campaign's primary enemy faction. The chapter is structured as a sustained combat sequence in the building's Janitor's Office, with the campaign's combat fundamentals fully introduced: the Service Weapon's multiple shape-shifting forms, Jesse's growing telekinetic abilities, and the campaign's signature destructible-environment combat.

The chapter introduces **Ahti**, the Oldest House's enigmatic Finnish janitor, who offers Jesse cryptic guidance in fragments of Finnish folk wisdom. Ahti's presence — and the broader Remedy Connected Universe references threaded through his dialogue — establish the campaign's metafictional foundation.

The chapter ends with Jesse's first sustained Hiss combat encounter cleared, the Service Weapon's first form unlocked, and the Bureau's central operational mystery introduced: what has Director Trench been concealing?

chapters.name: 3. Directorial Override
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first major dungeon. Jesse descends into the **Astral Plane**, an extradimensional space connected to the Oldest House through the Director's office. The chapter is structured as a sustained traversal through impossible geometries — floating platforms, vertical mazes, and the campaign's first introduction to the **Astral Plane challenge maps** that recur as side content.

The chapter unlocks Jesse's **Launch** ability — telekinetic projection of environmental objects — which becomes the campaign's most-used combat verb. The chapter weaves combat with environmental puzzles, with the destructible Brutalist architecture providing constant ammunition for Jesse's expanded toolkit.

The chapter ends with Jesse formally certified as the Bureau's Director by Polaris, her growing acceptance of the role increasingly visible, and the campaign's central mission — finding Dylan inside the Oldest House — fully staged.

chapters.name: 4. Threshold — Maintenance and Research
chapters.summary:
The campaign's mid-act exploration chapter. Jesse explores the **Maintenance** and **Research** sectors of the Oldest House, each rendered with significantly different atmospheric and architectural identities. The chapter weaves combat with the campaign's most sustained environmental storytelling — the Bureau's catalog of contained paranatural artifacts (the **Object of Power** archive), the audio logs of researchers describing their encounters with the Astral Plane, and the recurring file references to the **Altered World Events** that produced the artifacts themselves.

The chapter introduces the **Polaris connection** more explicitly. Polaris — the entity Jesse has carried inside her since childhood — is revealed as Jesse's connection to the Astral Plane and the source of her growing paranatural abilities. The chapter dwells on Jesse's growing comfort with the supernatural identity that has defined her since her brother's disappearance.

The chapter ends with Jesse acquiring the **Astral Plane traversal** ability, the Oldest House's full map now navigable, and the central question of Dylan's location finally focused.

chapters.name: 5. My Brother's Keeper
chapters.summary:
The campaign's emotional centerpiece. Jesse locates **Dylan Faden** — her missing brother — in a deep Bureau containment facility. He has been a Bureau prisoner for the past seventeen years, used as a test subject for paranatural research. The chapter dwells on the moral weight of the discovery: the Bureau Jesse has just inherited as Director has been systematically experimenting on her brother for nearly two decades.

The chapter is structured as a sustained conversation across the containment cell's barrier, with Dylan and Jesse reconstructing the events of their childhood that led to his Bureau captivity. The conversation reveals that the **Hiss** that has infected the Bureau may be related to the experiments performed on Dylan, and that Jesse's connection to Polaris may be the key to resolving both crises.

The chapter ends with Dylan possessed by the Hiss mid-conversation, the campaign's antagonist now clearly identified as Dylan's corrupted self, and Jesse's central mission reframed: saving the Bureau and saving her brother are now the same problem.

chapters.name: 6. The Face of the Enemy
chapters.summary:
The campaign's mid-third-act combat chapter. Jesse pursues evidence of the Hiss's origin through the Oldest House's deepest containment levels, with the chapter weaving combat against increasingly transformed Hiss variants with sustained environmental exploration. The chapter introduces several of the campaign's most acclaimed Object of Power encounters, including the **Refrigerator** (which must be continuously observed or it begins moving toward Jesse) and the **Floppy Disk** (which corrupts adjacent staff in escalating patterns).

The chapter dwells on the campaign's central metafictional layer — the Bureau's research papers reference *Alan Wake*'s Cauldron Lake, Bright Falls, and the Dark Place by name, establishing the Remedy Connected Universe as a direct continuity rather than a thematic overlap. The references seed the eventual *AWE* expansion's full cross-pollination.

The chapter ends with the campaign's first sustained boss confrontation against a Hiss-corrupted Bureau commander, Jesse's full ability roster now unlocked, and the Ashtray Maze sequence positioned for the next chapter.

chapters.name: 7. The Old Boys' Club — The Ashtray Maze
chapters.summary:
The most acclaimed single chapter in the campaign and one of the most acclaimed sequences in modern action gaming. The **Ashtray Maze** — a constantly reconfiguring labyrinthine corridor within the Oldest House — is staged as a sustained combat-and-traversal sequence accompanied by the Old Gods of Asgard song *Take Control*. The chapter is structured as a single continuous run through architecture that physically rearranges itself with every step.

The chapter's combat highlights include every system the campaign has taught: Service Weapon form-switching, Launch chains, dash-dodging, possessed-civilian saves, and the campaign's first sustained encounter with the **Former** — a paranatural entity that haunts the Maze's central chambers. The chapter's pacing has become one of the most cited examples of mechanical and narrative synthesis in modern game design.

The chapter ends with Jesse emerging from the Ashtray Maze, the path to the campaign's final mission unlocked, and the Hiss's central source — Dylan himself — now clearly the campaign's final boss.

chapters.name: 8. Polaris — The Final Confrontation
chapters.summary:
The campaign's climactic chapter. Jesse confronts **Dylan** in the deepest containment level of the Oldest House. The chapter is structured as a multi-stage boss fight, with Dylan's Hiss-possessed form weaving paranatural attacks that the campaign has been carefully escalating across every prior chapter. The fight ends with Jesse's full integration of Polaris's abilities — the campaign's most spectacle-heavy single encounter — and Dylan's containment in a Bureau-managed coma.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's central thematic resolution. Jesse has not "won" — the Bureau remains an institution that has experimented on her family, the Hiss remains a contained but unsolved problem, and the relationship with her brother is unresolved. The chapter ends with Jesse formally accepting the role of Director permanently and the credits rolling over a static shot of the Oldest House's exterior, with no exterior view of New York available.

chapters.name: 9. The Foundation (DLC)
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first expansion. **Marshall**, the Bureau's Head of Operations, sends Jesse beneath the Oldest House into the **Foundation** — the paranatural underground complex on which the Oldest House was built. The chapter is structured as a sustained vertical descent into Astral Plane-adjacent geology, with new combat scenarios and several new Service Weapon forms unlocked.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's mythology. The Foundation is the original Place of Power that the Bureau's headquarters was constructed to contain. The expansion's exposition deepens the campaign's relationship to Polaris, the Astral Plane, and the broader paranatural cosmology.

The chapter ends with the Foundation stabilized, a new Astral Plane breach contained, and the expansion's mid-campaign cliffhanger pointing toward *AWE*.

chapters.name: 10. AWE (DLC)
chapters.summary:
The campaign's second expansion and the full Remedy Connected Universe pivot. The Bureau's **Investigations Sector** opens, revealing files on the **Bright Falls AWE** — the Altered World Event that produced the events of *Alan Wake*. The chapter is structured as a sustained investigation of Bureau files crossing directly into Alan Wake's continuity, with Wake himself appearing in voice-over and recorded video segments.

The chapter's central confrontation is the **Hartman boss fight** — Doctor Emil Hartman, the antagonist of *Alan Wake*'s original DLC, manifesting in the Oldest House through the Hiss's connection to Cauldron Lake. The fight is the expansion's mechanical apex.

The chapter ends with the expansion's mid-credits scene setting up *Alan Wake II* in explicit terms: Alan Wake is alive, trapped in the Dark Place, writing the events of both *Control* and his own escape simultaneously. The Remedy Connected Universe is now formally a single continuity.

title: Doom: The Dark Ages
guide: Doom: The Dark Ages is id Software's prequel to Doom (2016) and Doom Eternal, set in a medieval techno-fantasy era of the Doom universe. The Slayer fights for the Maykrs in armies of demonic invaders across war-torn realms, wielding a redesigned Shield Saw, the Mace, and reimagined firearms. The walkthrough is a single full-game playthrough on PS5 Pro at 4K HDR 60FPS with PSSR upscaling, no commentary — pure cinematic carnage at PS5 Pro fidelity.

chapters.name: 1. Village of Khalim
chapters.summary:
The campaign opens with the **Doom Slayer** awakened from stasis on **Argent D'Nur** — the alien planet of the **Night Sentinels** — by the **Maykrs**, the angel-tier beings who control the Slayer's actions through the neural-control device called **the Tether**. The Slayer's first mission is the defense of the **Village of Khalim**, a Night Sentinel border settlement under active demonic invasion from Hell's expanding war.

The opening hour is structured as a sustained tutorial-paced introduction to the campaign's medieval-techno aesthetic and the franchise's reworked combat fundamentals. The Slayer's new combat repertoire is anchored by the **Shield Saw** — a saw-bladed shield that replaces *DOOM Eternal*'s shoulder-mounted Flame Belch as the campaign's primary defensive tool, supporting both parry mechanics and projectile defense. The chapter introduces parrying as the campaign's central combat verb, with green-flashing enemy projectiles signaling parry windows that turn defense into offensive opportunity.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's tonal pivot. Where *DOOM Eternal* was a high-mobility platforming-shooter hybrid, *The Dark Ages* is mechanically slower, with the Slayer rendered as an unstoppable tank rather than an acrobatic predator. The chapter ends with the Village of Khalim's beachhead secured, the demonic invasion's tactical objective destroyed, and the campaign's first sustained encounter against a **Titan**-class demon staged as the chapter's climactic boss fight.

chapters.name: 2. Hebeth
chapters.summary:
The campaign's second chapter takes the Slayer into **Hebeth's Weapons Facility**, a Night Sentinel manufacturing complex that has been compromised by demonic corruption from within. The chapter is structured as a multi-floor research-lab infiltration, with the campaign's first sustained interior environments and the introduction of additional weaponry — the **Combat Shotgun** with its parry-trigger reload, the **Pulverizer** with its grindable bone-fed ammunition, and the early versions of the campaign's signature **Skullcrusher** (a chaingun that fires skull fragments).

The chapter introduces the campaign's central environmental storytelling. Hebeth's facility logs reveal that the Maykrs' control over Night Sentinel R&D extends far beyond what King Novik's court openly acknowledges, with the early seeds of the campaign's central political conspiracy embedded in lab transcripts and recovered audio.

The chapter ends with the demonic corruption traced to its source in the facility's research basement, the Slayer recovering the **Shield Saw's** first major upgrade, and an Aggadon Champion confrontation staged as the chapter's climactic boss.

chapters.name: 3. Barrier Core
chapters.summary:
The campaign's most distinctive early-chapter design. The entire chapter is staged **inside the cockpit of the Atlan** — a massive Night Sentinel mech roughly the height of a skyscraper, deployed against demon Titans of similar scale. The chapter is structured as a sustained giant-mech combat sequence, with the Atlan's repertoire (missiles, shoulder-mounted artillery, melee combat) introduced through tutorial-paced encounters.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's scale shift. *DOOM* has never staged combat at this scale — the Atlan punches Titan-class demons through fortress walls, with city-block-sized impacts and destructible environmental geography. The mech missions become one of the campaign's most acclaimed design innovations.

The chapter ends with the Barrier Core's central reactor secured, the demon Titan threatening the region defeated, and the Slayer dismounting from the Atlan to continue on foot.

chapters.name: 4. Sentinel Barracks
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first chapter to introduce optional combat **challenges**. The Slayer arrives at a fortified Night Sentinel barracks complex under siege by a coordinated demonic assault force. The chapter is structured as a sustained defensive combat sequence across multiple barracks chambers, with the campaign's first sustained encounter with **Battle Knights** — heavily armored demon lieutenants that demand parry-and-counter mastery rather than direct-fire combat.

The chapter introduces the campaign's challenge system. Each challenge — killing forty demons, executing three-hit melee combos, finding hidden gold caches — unlocks weapon skins, ruby currency, and gold upgrades. The system encourages replays and provides the campaign's primary post-completion engagement loop.

The chapter ends with the barracks secured, the demonic assault repulsed, and the path to the campaign's central political setting opened.

chapters.name: 5. The Holy City of Aratum
chapters.summary:
The campaign's political center. **Aratum** is the capital city of the Night Sentinels and the seat of **King Novik**, the Sentinels' ceremonial monarch. The chapter is structured as a sustained urban-defense sequence, with the demon invasion penetrating the city's outer walls and the Slayer working alongside Night Sentinel forces to push them back. The chapter weaves the campaign's largest sustained combat scenarios with the introduction of the **Doomslayer's** parry-counter system's full repertoire.

The chapter introduces **Commander Thira**, the Night Sentinels' field commander and the campaign's primary supporting protagonist. Thira's introduction is staged through a sustained command-tent sequence in which she briefs the Slayer on the demonic forces' strategic objective: the **Heart of Argent**, an artifact of immense power housed within Aratum's central temple complex.

The chapter dwells on Aratum's architectural aesthetic. The medieval-techno fusion of Night Sentinel design — gothic cathedrals laced with energy conduits, banners hung over plasma turret emplacements — has become one of the campaign's most acclaimed visual achievements. The chapter ends with the city's outer defenses secured but the demonic invasion's broader objective unrevealed.

chapters.name: 6. Siege — Part 1
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first major spectacle chapter. **Prince Ahzrak**, the leader of Hell and the campaign's primary antagonist, launches a coordinated assault on Aratum with the explicit goal of seizing the Heart of Argent. The chapter is structured as a multi-stage defensive sequence — the Slayer mans a turret position to engage an armored Titan, falls back to the hangar to rescue King Novik, and conducts sustained urban combat across the city's collapsing infrastructure.

The chapter introduces **Prince Ahzrak** in person for the first time. Ahzrak is rendered as a tall, regal demon prince with deliberately elegant combat animations, contrasting sharply with the brute-force tone of the franchise's prior demon antagonists. His brief on-screen appearance is staged as a personal challenge to the Slayer, with Ahzrak vowing to acquire the Heart of Argent regardless of the Slayer's intervention.

The chapter ends with King Novik secured, the immediate demonic assault repulsed, and the broader siege now committed to its second phase.

chapters.name: 7. Siege — Part 2
chapters.summary:
The siege's second phase. The demonic forces, repulsed at the city's outer walls, breach through Aratum's underground tunnel network and emerge inside the city. The chapter is structured as a sustained interior combat sequence through the city's barracks complex, with the Slayer fighting to reach the **Forest Gate** that connects Aratum to the surrounding wilderness.

The chapter weaves combat with the campaign's most sustained Battle Knight encounters and the introduction of the **Aggadon Champion** — a recurring mid-tier boss class with sustained parry-and-counter mechanics. The chapter's climactic boss confrontation against an Aggadon Champion in the barracks' central armory is the campaign's first major mechanical test.

The chapter ends with the Forest Gate opened, the path to the **Mine Entrance** beyond it cleared, and the campaign's central thread now committed to the broader regional defense.

chapters.name: 8. Abyssal Forest
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first sustained natural-biome chapter. The Slayer enters the **Abyssal Forest**, a corrupted woodland where the demonic invasion has been operating from outside the Night Sentinels' surveillance. The chapter is structured as a sustained exploration sequence, with the campaign's most acclaimed map design — a multi-tier forest with hidden secrets, optional combat arenas, and the campaign's first sustained collectible density.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's environmental atmosphere. The Abyssal Forest is rendered in deep gold and purple tones, with twisted trees, demonic shrines, and the campaign's most acclaimed soundtrack cues. The combat density is moderate; the chapter's focus is exploration and the discovery of side content.

The chapter ends with the forest's central demonic shrine destroyed, the **Ancestral Heart** quest objective revealed, and the path to the Ancestral Forge opened.

chapters.name: 9. Ancestral Forge
chapters.summary:
The campaign's mid-game upgrade chapter. The Slayer activates the **Ancestral Heart** — a massive Night Sentinel forge powered by the same energy that fuels the Heart of Argent — and acquires his first **Shield Rune**, a permanent upgrade to the campaign's defensive repertoire. The chapter is structured as a multi-tier platform-traversal sequence with sustained combat against Hell's incoming reinforcements.

The chapter introduces the **Shield Rune** progression system. Each Shield Rune the Slayer acquires across the campaign expands the Shield Saw's repertoire — bash attacks, projectile-reflect modes, parry-trigger explosions, and the campaign's signature **Charged Lock-On** that allows the Slayer to fire concentrated energy attacks at distant enemies.

The chapter ends with the first Shield Rune acquired, the Ancestral Forge's secrets accessible for return visits, and the campaign's mid-act objective — the assault on **Ahzrak's Fortress** — fully staged.

chapters.name: 10. The Forsaken Plains
chapters.summary:
The campaign's largest open-zone chapter. The **Forsaken Plains** is a sweeping war-torn plateau between the Sentinel territories and Hell's encroaching forces. The chapter is structured as a sustained exploration sequence across one of the campaign's most expansive maps, with multiple optional combat arenas, hidden mini-bosses, and the campaign's most extensive collectible network.

The chapter dwells on the Slayer's growing tactical autonomy. The Maykrs' Tether control — established in the opening chapter as the Slayer's defining constraint — visibly weakens as the Slayer's growing combat experience strains its operational parameters. The chapter's audio logs reveal that the Maykrs themselves are growing concerned about the Slayer's behavioral drift.

The chapter ends with the Forsaken Plains secured, the path to **Ahzrak's Fortress** opened, and the campaign's mid-third-act assault committed.

chapters.name: 11. Hellbreaker
chapters.summary:
The campaign's second Atlan mech chapter. The Slayer pilots the giant mech across the **Forsaken Plains** toward Ahzrak's Fortress, with the chapter's combat staged at the same massive scale as Chapter 3. The chapter weaves Atlan combat with sustained on-foot infiltration sequences inside the fortress's interior chambers.

The chapter's central objective is the destruction of the **Hell Cannons** that protect the fortress's primary gate. The mech combat is the campaign's most sustained and one of its most acclaimed sequences. The chapter ends with the fortress breached, the Hell Cannons disabled, and the Slayer's first sustained on-foot infiltration into Hell's territory imminent.

chapters.name: 12. Sentinel Command Station
chapters.summary:
A perspective-shift chapter that returns to Aratum's territory after the Slayer's initial Hell incursion. The **Sentinel Command Station** has come under unexpected attack from a cosmic-scale entity — a paranatural creature that has emerged from outside the demon invasion's expected vectors. The chapter is structured as a sustained defensive sequence inside the station's multi-level interior.

The chapter introduces the campaign's first cosmic-scale enemy class. The cosmic creature attacks through demonic portals, tentacle weak spots, and zone-denial AOE attacks that demand the Slayer's full repertoire to manage. The chapter dwells on the campaign's escalating cosmology — the demon war is not the only threat to Argent D'Nur, and the broader paranatural framework of the *DOOM* universe is now visibly active.

The chapter ends with the cosmic creature defeated, the Sentinel Command Station secured, and the campaign's mid-third-act broader threats fully revealed.

chapters.name: 13. From Beyond
chapters.summary:
The campaign's lockdown-escape chapter. The Slayer awakens inside a Maykr-controlled facility, with the **Tether's control** visibly attempting to reassert dominance over his actions. The chapter is structured as a sustained interior infiltration with the campaign's first sustained encounter against Maykr-controlled enforcers.

The chapter's central objective is to **find a way out of the lockdown** and reach **Serrat**, the Slayer's cyber-dragon companion now revealed for the first time. The chapter weaves combat with environmental traversal across the facility's interior, with the **Hangar Key Card** acquisition as the chapter's central progression objective.

The chapter ends with Serrat liberated, the dragon-mount traversal system fully unlocked, and the campaign's broader aerial-combat repertoire now active. The chapter's quiet revelation is that the Slayer has been a Maykr captive for longer than the campaign's opening framing implied.

chapters.name: 14. Spire of Nerathul
chapters.summary:
The campaign's central pivot chapter. The Slayer pilots **Serrat** to the **Spire of Nerathul** — a massive Old One artifact located in a contested cosmic zone — to **drain the water levels and deactivate the Stasis Emitters** that have been suppressing the spire's core. The chapter is structured as a multi-stage puzzle-combat sequence with sustained dragon-combat aerial set-pieces.

The chapter's central objective is the acquisition of the **BFC** (Big Cosmic something — the campaign's parallel to the franchise's iconic BFG) and the release of **The Old One** — an ancient being who has been imprisoned beneath the spire across centuries. The Old One's release becomes the campaign's mid-third-act pivot.

The chapter ends with the BFC acquired, The Old One released, and the path to **Thira's** location in the **Cosmic Realm** now clear.

chapters.name: 15. City of Ry'uul
chapters.summary:
A multi-island exploration chapter set across the cosmic-realm city of **Ry'uul**. The chapter is structured as a sustained exploration sequence across several interconnected island districts, with the campaign's most acclaimed environmental storytelling and the introduction of new enemy variants drawn from the cosmic-realm's distinct enemy bestiary.

The chapter's central objective is the gathering of **Relic Fragments** scattered across the city's districts, which the Slayer must assemble to reach the **Temple of Lomarith** in a later chapter. The chapter dwells on the cosmic realm's aesthetic — a fusion of medieval Sentinel architecture with cosmic-scale geometries that the campaign has been building toward since the Sentinel Command Station.

The chapter ends with the Relic Fragments collected, the path to the Temple of Lomarith mapped, and the campaign's broader cosmic-realm questline fully committed.

chapters.name: 16. The Kar'Thul Marshes
chapters.summary:
A sustained natural-biome chapter set in the **Kar'Thul Marshes** — a corrupted wetland region between the cosmic realm and the demonic territories. The chapter is structured as a multi-zone exploration sequence with the campaign's hardest sustained combat encounters and the introduction of new enemy variants adapted to the marsh's waterlogged terrain.

The chapter weaves combat with sustained environmental puzzles, including water-level manipulation and the campaign's most distinctive parry-and-traversal combinations. The chapter ends with the marshes traversed, the path to the Temple of Lomarith now directly accessible, and the campaign's third-act assault on Hell's leadership now positioned.

chapters.name: 17. Temple of Lomarith
chapters.summary:
The campaign's mid-third-act ancient-temple chapter. The **Temple of Lomarith** is a pre-Sentinel cosmic-realm structure that contains key intelligence on Prince Ahzrak's actual objective. The chapter is structured as a sustained interior exploration with the campaign's most acclaimed puzzle sequences and the introduction of the **Witch** — the campaign's secondary antagonist who has been operating alongside Ahzrak in the shadows.

The chapter's central reveal is the Heart of Argent's true power source: **Commander Thira** carries the soul of **Wraith**, an ancient being whose fragmented essence is the Heart's actual operational core. Thira's role in the campaign is reframed — she is not merely Aratum's field commander but the campaign's central macguffin.

The chapter ends with the Temple of Lomarith's intelligence recovered, the Witch's identity confirmed, and the campaign's final assault on Hell's command structure committed.

chapters.name: 18. Belly of the Beast
chapters.summary:
The campaign's most distinctive single-chapter design. The Slayer is swallowed by a massive ancient cosmic beast — an enemy class the campaign has staged across previous chapters at smaller scale — and the entire chapter takes place inside the creature's body. The chapter is structured as a sustained interior-organic exploration, with the campaign's most acclaimed environmental design and the introduction of biological combat scenarios distinct from anything in the franchise's prior installments.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's tonal commitment. Where prior chapters have weighed combat against environmental texture, "Belly of the Beast" foregrounds the campaign's most experimental design choices — destructible biological structures, ammunition synthesized from the creature's interior fluids, and the chapter's climactic escape sequence staged as the Slayer carves his way out from inside.

The chapter ends with the cosmic beast destroyed from within, the Slayer ejected back into the campaign's geography, and the path to the campaign's emotional pivot chapter — **Harbor of Souls** — now clear.

chapters.name: 19. Harbor of Souls
chapters.summary:
The campaign's most thematically loaded chapter. The Slayer descends into a representation of the **afterlife** — a cosmic-realm zone between life and death — in pursuit of essential knowledge before the final assault. The chapter is structured as a sustained narrative-led exploration with reduced combat density and the campaign's most extensive lore reveals.

The chapter's central reveal is the broader campaign cosmology. The Slayer is not merely a Maykr-controlled super-weapon. He is an ancient being whose history predates the current war, whose memory has been suppressed by the Maykrs' Tether across multiple resurrections, and whose true origin connects to the broader paranatural framework of the *DOOM* universe. The chapter dwells on this reveal in extended cutscene sequences.

The chapter ends with the Slayer's broader history restored to his memory, the Tether's control permanently broken, and the campaign's final assault now committed with the protagonist fully autonomous.

chapters.name: 20. Resurrection
chapters.summary:
The campaign's third-act pivot. The Slayer, now fully free from Maykr control, returns to Argent D'Nur to find the Night Sentinels in catastrophic retreat. Ahzrak, having captured Commander Thira and extracted the Wraith's soul from her, has weaponized the Heart of Argent's full power for a final assault on the Sentinels. **Kreed Maykr**, revealed as a traitor working in coordination with Ahzrak, has accelerated the betrayal.

The chapter is structured as a sustained defensive combat sequence across Aratum's collapsing infrastructure, with the Slayer pushing back the demonic assault to repel the immediate threat. The chapter weaves combat with the campaign's most acclaimed environmental destruction sequences, with much of Aratum's signature architecture visibly collapsing across the chapter's runtime.

The chapter's mid-section boss fight is the confrontation with **Kreed Maykr**, the traitor whose betrayal accelerated Hell's advance. The fight is the campaign's most personal antagonist encounter, with Kreed wielding Maykr technology against the Slayer he once controlled. The chapter ends with Kreed defeated, the immediate Aratum defense secured, and the path to Ahzrak's final stand opened.

chapters.name: 21. Final Battle
chapters.summary:
The campaign's penultimate chapter. The Slayer pilots the **Atlan mech** for the third and final time, breaching Ahzrak's central fortress for the campaign's climactic boss confrontation. The chapter is structured as a multi-stage assault — mech combat against the fortress's defensive infrastructure, on-foot infiltration through the interior, and the climactic first phase of the Ahzrak boss fight.

The Ahzrak fight is the campaign's mechanical apex. The Prince of Hell's combat repertoire combines every enemy archetype the campaign has staged across the prior twenty chapters — Battle Knight melee, Titan-scale projectile attacks, Witch-affiliated dark magic — into a sustained boss confrontation. Ahzrak resurrects multiple times during the fight, each time reframing his combat patterns and forcing the player to recalibrate.

The chapter ends with Ahzrak temporarily defeated, Thira's soul released from the Heart of Argent, and Hell's immediate momentum crippled. The chapter explicitly refuses to call the campaign concluded — Ahzrak's broader resurrection remains active, and the campaign's final confrontation is staged for the closing chapter.

chapters.name: 22. Reckoning
chapters.summary:
The campaign's climactic chapter. The Slayer enters the **Hell dimension itself** for the final confrontation with **Prince Ahzrak and the Witch together**. The chapter is structured as a sustained boss-rush sequence inside Hell's most distinctive arenas, with the combined Ahzrak-Witch fight serving as the campaign's mechanical and thematic apex.

The combined boss confrontation is one of the franchise's most acclaimed encounters. Ahzrak and the Witch fight in coordinated patterns, with the Witch providing dark-magic support to Ahzrak's melee assaults while Ahzrak's resurrection mechanics force the Slayer to balance damage between the two antagonists. The fight ends with **Ahzrak decapitated** by the Slayer in a sequence staged with the franchise's signature brutal finisher animation.

The chapter's epilogue is staged in the aftermath. **Thira and her Sentinel forces** — surrounded by an overwhelming demonic army — receive the Slayer's grim reaper-style intervention as he throws **Ahzrak's decapitated head** down from above into the demonic formation, breaking their advance. The war is won. The Maykrs publicly disavow Kreed's betrayal, but the damage has been done — Thira urges the surviving Night Sentinels to trust only in themselves going forward.

The chapter's final scene establishes the campaign's connection to the broader franchise. The Slayer, now fully free from Maykr control, takes Kreed's ship and departs Argent D'Nur on his own personal crusade against Hell — the direct setup for *DOOM* (2016)'s opening hour. The credits roll over the Slayer's departure, with the campaign's central question — whether one being can truly end the eternal war — answered in the only way the franchise permits: through sustained, individual, brutal commitment.

title: Dying Light 2: Stay Human
guide: Dying Light 2: Stay Human is Techland's open-world parkour-zombie sequel set 15 years after the original. Aiden Caldwell, a Pilgrim, arrives in the City — one of the last human settlements — searching for his missing sister. The walkthrough captures the full main campaign across two parts on PS5 Pro at 4K HDR 60FPS with PSSR upscaling, no commentary. Both day and night gameplay loops are preserved with their distinct combat tones.

chapters.name: 1. The Pilgrim — Aiden Arrives
chapters.summary:
The campaign opens twenty-two years after the original *Dying Light*, with the world having fully collapsed under the **Harran Virus** pandemic. The protagonist **Aiden Caldwell** — a "Pilgrim," a member of the post-collapse traveler caste who carries messages and goods between the few surviving urban settlements — approaches the campaign's central city, **Villedor**, in pursuit of his missing sister **Mia**.

The opening sequence is structured as a sustained tutorial-paced introduction to the campaign's reworked parkour fundamentals. *Dying Light 2*'s movement system is significantly more refined than the original game's — wall-running, dynamic vaulting, paragliding, and the franchise's signature day-night mechanics are all reintroduced through the opening chapter's exploration of Villedor's outer ruins.

The chapter ends with Aiden's arrival at Villedor's central gate, the campaign's central conflict (Aiden's infection, his pursuit of the man named **Waltz** who tortured him and his sister as children) introduced, and the city's interior districts opened to exploration.

chapters.name: 2. Old Villedor — The Bazaar
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first sustained social-investigation chapter. Aiden enters **Old Villedor**, the city's eastern district, controlled by the **Survivors** faction — a community of post-collapse civilians organized around mutual support and democratic governance. The chapter is structured as a sustained exploration of the **Bazaar**, the district's central marketplace, with the campaign's first sustained character-introduction sequences.

The chapter introduces several of the campaign's primary supporting characters: **Hakon**, an enigmatic information broker; **Aitor**, the Bazaar's elected leader; **Sophie**, a Survivor militia commander; and **Frank**, Sophie's brother and Aiden's first stable contact. The chapter dwells on the franchise's central political dynamic — the **Survivors** faction's idealism contrasts with the **Peacekeepers** faction's authoritarian pragmatism, and the campaign's choice architecture is built around the player's accumulated faction preferences.

The chapter ends with Aiden establishing his temporary base in the Bazaar, the campaign's central social network mapped, and the path to Hakon's intelligence on Waltz committed.

chapters.name: 3. The Bazaar Assault — First Major Encounter
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first sustained combat chapter. The Bazaar comes under coordinated assault by **Renegades**, a faction of post-collapse raiders operating from the city's southern industrial districts. The chapter is structured as a sustained defensive combat sequence weaving combat against human enemies with the franchise's signature infected combat fundamentals.

The chapter introduces the campaign's signature **day-night dynamic** at full operational intensity. Aiden's combat capability is significantly stronger during daylight hours; nighttime introduces the campaign's most dangerous infected variants — **Volatiles** that hunt by sound and movement — alongside the night-only stamina regeneration mechanic that allows the player to grind out infected combat encounters specifically during darkness.

The chapter ends with the Bazaar's defense secured, the campaign's central social network reinforced, and the path to the city's central districts opened.

chapters.name: 4. The Central Loop — Crossing the Bridge
chapters.summary:
The campaign's mid-act geography expansion. Aiden completes a multi-stage investigation that culminates in his crossing **the Bridge** — Villedor's signature parkour set-piece — into **the Central Loop**, the city's western district controlled by the rival **Peacekeepers** faction. The chapter is structured as a sustained vertical traversal sequence with the campaign's most acclaimed parkour combat encounters.

The chapter introduces the campaign's central political crisis. The Central Loop's Peacekeepers, led by Colonel **Williams**, operate under a significantly more authoritarian framework — public executions of infected suspects, mandatory infection-status checks at district boundaries, and the franchise's most morally compromised central command structure. The chapter dwells on Aiden's choice between supporting the Peacekeepers' more functional but ethically suspect governance or the Survivors' more democratic but militarily vulnerable approach.

The chapter ends with Aiden's first major faction-allegiance decision required, the Central Loop's exploration loop fully active, and the campaign's central political conflict committed.

chapters.name: 5. The Markers of Plague — Hakon's Betrayal
chapters.summary:
The campaign's mid-game pivot chapter. **Hakon** — the information broker who has been guiding Aiden through the city's central conspiracies — is revealed as having a longer history with **Waltz**, Aiden's central antagonist, than he has admitted. The chapter is structured as a sustained pursuit-and-confrontation sequence across the city's rooftop network, with Hakon attempting to escape Aiden's confrontation through the franchise's most acclaimed sustained chase sequence.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's central character pivot. Hakon's complicated relationship to Waltz — partner, victim, and reluctant collaborator across multiple decades — reframes the campaign's central conflict from straightforward revenge into something significantly more morally compromised.

The chapter ends with Hakon's apparent death (the player's choice can affect this), Waltz's location confirmed in the city's central administrative complex, and the campaign's third-act pursuit committed.

chapters.name: 6. The Cathedral — Waltz's Sanctuary
chapters.summary:
The campaign's third-act setting. **The Cathedral** — the city's central religious complex, converted into Waltz's personal research facility — is rendered as the campaign's most architecturally ambitious environment. The chapter is structured as a sustained infiltration sequence with the campaign's most demanding sustained combat encounters.

The chapter introduces the campaign's central scientific subplot. Waltz has been conducting experimental research on a potential cure for the Harran Virus across decades, with the cathedral's research wings revealing both genuine scientific breakthroughs and significant ethical violations. The chapter dwells on Aiden's growing recognition that **Mia** — his sister, whom he has been searching for since the opening chapter — has been one of Waltz's primary research subjects across the intervening years.

The chapter ends with Mia located in the cathedral's central chamber, Waltz's confrontation with Aiden positioned, and the campaign's emotional climax committed.

chapters.name: 7. The Waltz Confrontation
chapters.summary:
The campaign's climactic chapter. **Waltz**, the man who tortured Aiden and Mia as children, confronts Aiden inside the cathedral's research wing. The chapter is structured as a sustained dialogue-and-combat sequence, with the campaign's central revelation about Waltz's actual identity and motivations delivered across multiple narrative beats.

The chapter's central reveal is that **Waltz is Aiden and Mia's biological father**. The "torturer" framing across the campaign's prior chapters has been Aiden's traumatized misremembering of childhood scientific experimentation Waltz conducted on his own children — experiments that Waltz framed at the time as essential medical research, and that Mia has spent the intervening years either forgiving or refusing to forgive depending on the player's prior choices.

The chapter ends with Waltz's defeat or escape (depending on player choices), the campaign's central family revelation processed, and the campaign's final moral decision staged.

chapters.name: 8. The Final Choice — Ending Configurations
chapters.summary:
The campaign's climactic decision chapter. Aiden, having confronted Waltz and reunited with Mia, faces the campaign's central political decision: **who controls the city after the crisis is resolved?** The player's accumulated faction-allegiance choices across the campaign determine the available endings, with the chapter staged as a sustained set of dialogue-and-action decisions across the city's central infrastructure.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's central thematic argument. Villedor's survival requires governance, and no governance configuration is morally clean. The chapter's possible endings include **Survivors-controlled democracy** (idealistic but vulnerable), **Peacekeepers-controlled authoritarianism** (stable but morally compromised), **Renegades-controlled anarchy** (visceral but unsustainable), and several hybrid configurations depending on the player's specific accumulated choices.

The chapter ends with the player's chosen ending played out across the city's epilogue cinematics, with the campaign's central question — what kind of city can survive the post-collapse world — answered through the player's accumulated political commitments rather than through any single triumphant resolution.

chapters.name: 9. Epilogue — Aiden Departs
chapters.summary:
The campaign's epilogue chapter is staged as a quiet sustained sequence with no combat. Aiden, Mia reunited, prepares to depart Villedor on a new Pilgrim contract — the city's broader regional network has been disrupted by Waltz's research operations, and the surviving Pilgrim community needs Aiden's continued commitment to maintain communication between the few surviving settlements across the post-collapse landscape.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's character resolutions. Mia's recovery is rendered with deliberate ambiguity — she is alive, she has reconnected with her brother, but the years of Waltz's research have left permanent psychological consequences that the campaign refuses to soften. The chapter ends with Aiden's departure from Villedor, the city's chosen governance configuration visible across the cinematic, and the campaign's central question — whether the post-collapse world is salvageable — answered with the franchise's signature qualified hope.

The credits roll over Aiden's solitary departure into the surrounding ruins, with the post-launch expansion content (**Bloody Ties**, **Tower Raid**) explicitly seeded as the franchise's continuation.

title: Echoes of the End
guide: Echoes of the End is an Icelandic action-adventure from Myrkur Games following Ryn, a former soldier whose latent magical abilities draw her into a continent-spanning conspiracy alongside companion Abram. Combat blends sword strikes with elemental abilities — telekinesis, ranged blasts, environmental manipulation. The world design draws heavily on Icelandic landscape inspiration, with volcanic ridges and frost-bitten coastlines as the backdrop. The walkthrough captures the full game across two parts on PS5 Pro at 4K HDR 60FPS with PSSR upscaling, no commentary.

chapters.name: 1. The Awakening — Ryn's Journey Begins
chapters.summary:
The campaign opens with **Ryn** — the protagonist, a young woman with latent magical abilities — awakening in the fantasy world of **Aema**, a Norse-influenced landscape designed by the Icelandic developer Myrkur Games as a fusion of medieval European and ancient Nordic aesthetics. The opening sequence is structured as a sustained tutorial-paced introduction to the campaign's reworked traversal fundamentals, with Ryn navigating early forest environments while the player learns the campaign's signature climbing-and-platforming mechanics.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's tonal commitment. Aema is rendered with significantly more environmental texture than typical fantasy-RPG opening environments — extensive ambient soundscapes, painterly visual design, and the campaign's signature attention to natural-world detail. The chapter introduces the campaign's central magic mechanic: **the Shift**, a telekinetic ability that allows Ryn to manipulate objects (and eventually enemies) across distance.

The chapter ends with Ryn's first sustained combat encounter — a tutorial-paced introduction to the campaign's parry-and-counter combat fundamentals — and the path to the central village committed.

chapters.name: 2. The Companion — Meeting Abram
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first sustained character-introduction chapter. **Abram**, an older companion-figure with significant prior experience with the campaign's central conflict, joins Ryn at the chapter's mid-section. The chapter is structured as a sustained partnership-building sequence weaving combat against the campaign's first sustained encounters with **Glómori** — the corrupted-creature antagonist class that has been infesting Aema's countryside.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's central character dynamic. Abram and Ryn's partnership is rendered with significantly more conversational warmth than typical fantasy-game companion relationships — sustained casual dialogue, mutual problem-solving across early traversal puzzles, and the franchise's signature optional-conversation system that allows the player to invest in their developing relationship.

The chapter ends with Ryn and Abram committing to a shared journey, the campaign's central antagonist faction (the corruption infrastructure causing the Glómori manifestations) introduced through environmental detail, and the path deeper into Aema's broader geography opened.

chapters.name: 3. The Durtar — Ancient Artifacts
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first sustained worldbuilding chapter. **The Durtar** are an ancient pre-collapse civilization whose surviving artifacts — recovered across the chapter's exploration — provide the campaign's primary lore foundation. The chapter is structured as a sustained exploration sequence through ruined Durtar settlements with significantly increased combat density.

The chapter weaves combat with the franchise's signature collectibles-and-conversation infrastructure. Journal entries recovered across Durtar sites provide the campaign's most thematically loaded historical context, with the player's accumulated reading material gradually exposing the broader mythological framework underlying the campaign's present-day conflicts.

The chapter ends with Ryn's growing understanding of her own connection to the Durtar civilization, the campaign's central magical-inheritance subplot fully active, and the path to the campaign's first major boss encounter committed.

chapters.name: 4. The First Awakening — Powers Develop
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first sustained character-development chapter. Ryn's latent magical abilities — initially limited to basic Shift telekinesis — manifest at significantly higher operational power. The chapter is structured as a sustained exploration sequence with the franchise's first sustained Double-Jump-enabled traversal puzzles, weaving the campaign's expanded movement repertoire with combat against significantly more demanding Glómori variants.

The chapter dwells on Ryn's character arc. Her growing combat capability, her expanding understanding of her own magical heritage, and her deepening partnership with Abram all anchor the chapter's emotional weight. The chapter ends with Ryn's full Shift repertoire unlocked, the campaign's central magical-power-progression system fully active, and the path to the campaign's first sustained antagonist encounter committed.

chapters.name: 5. Zara — The Antagonist Appears
chapters.summary:
The campaign's central antagonist-introduction chapter. **Zara** — a more powerful magic-user with personal connections to Ryn's family history — confronts Ryn and Abram in a sustained cutscene-and-combat sequence at the chapter's mid-section. The chapter is structured as a sustained character-confrontation chapter weaving exposition with combat across the chapter's runtime.

The chapter dwells on Zara's character. She is rendered with significantly more emotional complexity than typical fantasy-RPG antagonists, with her grievances against Ryn's family rooted in personal trauma that the campaign treats with sustained earnestness. The chapter introduces the campaign's central antagonist motivation — Zara's pursuit of an ancient power source she believes will restore something that was taken from her.

The chapter ends with Zara temporarily withdrawing, the campaign's central mid-act pursuit dynamic established, and the path to the campaign's volcanic-region exploration opened.

chapters.name: 6. The Volcanic Region — The Dash Ability
chapters.summary:
The campaign's most visually distinctive single chapter. The **volcanic region** is rendered as the campaign's most thermally intense environment, with sustained traversal across lava-flow geography and the franchise's most acclaimed environmental hazards. The chapter introduces the campaign's signature **Dash** ability — a quick-movement traversal option that significantly expands Ryn's combat-and-traversal repertoire.

The chapter is structured as a sustained exploration sequence with the campaign's first sustained encounter with the **Eobard** boss. Eobard — a fallen ruler whose throne the player can subsequently sit on after the encounter — is rendered with significantly more environmental gravitas than the campaign's prior antagonists. The fight is the campaign's first sustained mechanical apex, demanding mastery of Ryn's full combat repertoire across the encounter's multiple phases.

The chapter ends with Eobard defeated, the volcanic region's central artifacts recovered, and the path to the campaign's mid-third-act pivot opened.

chapters.name: 7. The Separation — Abram Lost
chapters.summary:
The campaign's emotional pivot chapter. Following a sustained combat encounter in the campaign's mid-third act, Ryn is separated from Abram by an environmental catastrophe. The chapter is structured as a sustained solo-exploration sequence with significantly reduced combat density, dedicated entirely to Ryn's character arc and her growing self-sufficiency.

The chapter dwells on Ryn's character development. Her capacity to function independently of Abram's mentorship — a question the campaign's prior chapters have left deliberately open — is fully answered through the chapter's sustained solo investigation sequences. The chapter ends with Ryn's reunion with Abram, the partnership reframed as a relationship between equals rather than between mentor and student, and the path to the campaign's third-act revelations committed.

chapters.name: 8. The Brother Revealed
chapters.summary:
The campaign's central reveal chapter. **Ryn's brother** — referenced through environmental detail across the prior chapters — is revealed as significantly more central to the campaign's underlying conflict than the player has been led to believe. The chapter is structured as a sustained dialogue-and-investigation sequence with the campaign's most acclaimed environmental storytelling.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's central family history. Ryn's brother, her family's broader magical inheritance, and Zara's personal connection to all of the above are reframed across the chapter's narrative beats. The campaign's central political question — whether Zara's grievances are legitimate or whether her methods invalidate them — is rendered with significantly more moral complexity than the campaign's prior characterization implied.

The chapter ends with the campaign's central mystery fully resolved, the path to Zara's final confrontation committed, and the campaign's third-act setup positioned.

chapters.name: 9. The Water Traversal — Boat Navigation
chapters.summary:
The campaign's most acclaimed single traversal chapter. Ryn's pursuit of Zara across Aema's broader geography requires sustained boat-based navigation across the campaign's most expansive water-environment sequences. The chapter is structured as a sustained exploration sequence weaving boat-traversal combat with the campaign's most acclaimed environmental design.

The chapter weaves combat against waterborne Glómori variants with sustained environmental investigation of Aema's surviving coastal settlements. The chapter's central event is the recovery of critical intelligence regarding Zara's central operational base, with the campaign's final assault positioned.

chapters.name: 10. The Final Confrontation — Zara
chapters.summary:
