Peter's day job — working as a research assistant for **Dr. Otto Octavius** at Octavius Industries — is introduced in full, with the prosthetic-arm puzzle that doubles as the campaign's first lab minigame. The neural-prosthetic project that Otto and Peter are working on is positioned as an act of genuine scientific idealism — Otto wants to help amputees regain function, and the project is genuinely revolutionary.

The chapter's central conflict is **Mayor Norman Osborn**'s political pressure on Otto. Osborn, Otto's former research partner, has just defunded the entire project, and his on-camera explanation — broadcast in news clips throughout Manhattan — frames Otto as a failed scientist who could not deliver results. Otto's quiet response, staged in the lab's cracked-window office, is one of the campaign's most carefully written scenes.

The chapter introduces the **circuit puzzle** and **molecule synthesis** minigames that will recur throughout the campaign's collectibles and research stations. Peter's growing trust in Otto, his sense of obligation, and his gradual realization that Otto is starting to break — all are seeded here. The chapter ends with Peter walking out of the lab and back into his Spider-Man life, with the campaign's tragedy already underway behind him.

chapters.name: 5. The First Negative Encounter
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first direct confrontation with **Mister Negative**. The chapter is staged across a multi-block Demon assault in midtown, with corrupted-energy attacks tearing through Times Square and Spider-Man arriving to find a fully transformed Negative leading the assault personally. The level design escalates from open-world traversal to a closed-arena boss fight against Negative at the chapter's midpoint.

The first Mister Negative encounter is mechanically designed to be unwinnable — Negative teleports, throws corrupted-energy waves, summons Demon reinforcements faster than Spider-Man can clear them, and ultimately escapes after a punishing exchange. The fight is staged to teach the player that Negative is not a normal villain. The player is expected to lose ground throughout, and the cutscene resolution explicitly frames Spider-Man as outmatched.

The chapter's closing minutes pivot to the **Black Cat stakeouts** — Felicia Hardy's calling cards begin appearing on rooftops, planting the seeds of the post-game DLC arc. The chapter ends with Yuri Watanabe demanding Spider-Man take Negative seriously, and Peter walking out of the precinct with Mister Negative now on his personal hunt list.

chapters.name: 6. F.E.A.S.T. and Miles Morales
chapters.summary:
A narrative-focused chapter that introduces two of the campaign's most important supporting characters. The first is **Martin Li**, the philanthropist who founded the F.E.A.S.T. homeless shelter where Aunt May volunteers — the player has already encountered Mister Negative, and the chapter dwells in the dramatic-irony space of knowing that the soft-spoken Li is the man Spider-Man has been hunting. The shelter scenes are staged with warmth, including a quiet conversation between Peter and May about whether she should retire from active volunteer work.

The chapter's second introduction is **Miles Morales**, the teenager whose family Aunt May has been mentoring through F.E.A.S.T.'s programs. Miles's father, **Officer Jefferson Davis**, is introduced as a working-class NYPD cop with deep loyalty to the shelter and to Mayor Osborn's reformist policies. The Davis family is established as decent, working-class, and quietly important. The chapter does not yet kill any of them.

A side mission inside the chapter has Spider-Man tracking down a warehouse fire that turns out to be a Demon attack on a F.E.A.S.T. supply depot. The fight is the chapter's combat centerpiece. The chapter closes with Peter introducing himself to Miles at the shelter as Aunt May's "friend Peter," and the audience knowing what Miles will eventually become.

chapters.name: 7. Marked Man
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first major narrative pivot. Mister Negative launches a public, coordinated attack on a political event hosted by Mayor Norman Osborn. The chapter's central set-piece is staged across the rooftops surrounding the event and on the public stage itself. Mister Negative's grievance is finally articulated in full — a years-old Oscorp experiment ruined Li's family, and Li blames Osborn personally. The attack is intended to kill Osborn on live television.

The chapter's centerpiece is the **second Mister Negative duel**, fought on an open-air rooftop against the falling debris of the event. Negative is faster, his corrupted-energy walls cut through the arena, and his charged attacks demand perfect-dodge timing the campaign has only just begun to teach. The fight ends mid-sequence as Negative breaks off to commit the chapter's defining act.

That act is the **death of Officer Jefferson Davis**. Davis is killed protecting Osborn during the public attack, in a sequence staged with deliberate weight and no music. The death is the campaign's emotional pivot — it transforms Miles Morales's family from background detail into the campaign's central tragedy, and it commits the player to whatever the next act delivers. The chapter ends with Aunt May breaking the news to Miles's mother **Rio** at F.E.A.S.T., and the city in mourning.

chapters.name: 8. Devil's Breath Unleashed
chapters.summary:
The chapter that opens Act 3. Mister Negative, his political assassination foiled, pivots to the larger plan he has been preparing the entire campaign: the release of **Devil's Breath**, an Oscorp-engineered bioweapon, into Manhattan's water supply. The chapter is structured as a citywide crisis sequence, with Spider-Man racing across districts trying to contain the initial release while the death toll climbs in news broadcasts.

The chapter intercuts Peter's Spider-Man work with Otto Octavius's parallel descent. Otto, devastated by Osborn's betrayal and the failure of his life's work, has been quietly building four mechanical tentacles in his off-campus workshop. The tentacle prototypes are revealed in a sequence staged in a half-lit garage, with Otto's voice still soft, still measured, still recognizably Peter's mentor — and clearly preparing to do something terrible.

The chapter introduces **Aunt May's infection**. Working at F.E.A.S.T. during the initial release, May is one of thousands exposed before the public quarantine. Her diagnosis is delivered in a hospital scene that the campaign will pay off explicitly in its final hour. The chapter closes with the city under martial law, Mister Negative still at large, and Otto's transformation complete.

chapters.name: 9. The Raft Breakout
chapters.summary:
The campaign's most acclaimed mid-game sequence. Doctor Octopus — Otto Octavius now fully transformed — breaks into **The Raft**, the maximum-security island prison off the coast of Manhattan, and releases the **Sinister Six**: Electro, Vulture, Rhino, Scorpion, and Mister Negative. The chapter is structured as a continuous descent through the Raft's containment levels, with each Sinister Six member introduced via their breakout cinematic.

The chapter's defining moment is Spider-Man's first encounter with Doctor Octopus. The fight is mechanically scripted — Doc Ock has total environmental control, can throw concrete pillars at Spider-Man, and is functionally invulnerable to direct attack. The player is forced to lose. The cinematic that follows the staged defeat — **Otto's "I've known all along"** identity reveal — is one of the franchise's defining moments. The reveal recontextualizes the entire campaign: every mentor scene, every lab session, every quiet conversation has been with a man who has been planning this.

The chapter closes with the Sinister Six scattering across Manhattan, the city's largest threat in decades unleashed, and Spider-Man broken on the Raft floor with no clear plan for what comes next.

chapters.name: 10. The Sinister Six Hunt
chapters.summary:
The campaign's mid-third-act open hunt. Spider-Man recovers, returns to Manhattan, and begins hunting the Sinister Six members across the city. The chapter is structured as a hub mission with multiple paths — the player can engage the villains in any order, though the campaign nudges toward a specific sequence.

The chapter's two major boss encounters are the tag-team fights with **Electro and Vulture** and with **Rhino and Scorpion**. The Electro/Vulture duel is staged in Times Square at night, with Vulture using the city's vertical space and Electro chaining lightning across the ground. The Rhino/Scorpion fight takes place in the Financial District, with Rhino's charge attacks forcing the player to learn environmental staggers and Scorpion's hallucinogenic venom distorting the screen with each hit. Both fights are designed to feel like genuine super-villain confrontations rather than reskinned grunt encounters.

The chapter closes with four of the six Sinister Six members in custody, Negative and Doc Ock still at large, and Aunt May's condition worsening at the hospital. The campaign begins its march toward the finale.

chapters.name: 11. The Heart of the Matter
chapters.summary:
The final Mister Negative encounter. The chapter opens with Spider-Man tracking Negative through a citywide helicopter chase, with corrupted-energy attacks raining from the helicopter and the player pursuing in pure swing-traversal mode. The chase ends on a rooftop arena, where Negative makes his final stand in a three-phase boss fight.

The fight is staged across a vertical arena, with each phase forcing the player to relearn Negative's combo strings. Phase 1 is the corrupted-energy moveset the campaign has been teaching since Act 1; phase 2 accelerates everything; phase 3 transforms Negative into a full Inner Demon form, with full-screen AOE attacks that punish anything but perfect-dodge mastery. The fight is the campaign's most pure mechanical exam outside the final battle.

The chapter closes with Negative defeated and Martin Li, finally stripped of his powers, broken on the rooftop. The man Aunt May still believes is a hero is taken into custody, and Peter — for the first time in the campaign — fully understands what the death of Officer Davis cost, and what kind of cost the campaign is still going to demand of him.

chapters.name: 12. The Oscorp Tower Battle
chapters.summary:
The campaign's climactic confrontation. **Doctor Octopus** has barricaded himself at the top of Oscorp Tower with the only doses of antiserum to the Devil's Breath pathogen — including the one that could save Aunt May. The chapter is structured as a vertical assault: Spider-Man fights his way up the tower against Doc Ock's tentacle traps and Sinister Six remnants, then engages Otto himself in the campaign's signature multi-phase boss fight.

The Doc Ock final battle is one of the most acclaimed boss encounters of the past console generation. Stage 1 is staged on the upper terrace with Otto on the ground, his tentacles slamming the arena and creating windows for Spider-Man to web-strike each tentacle when it embeds. Stage 2 transitions into a vertical tower-climb sequence, with Spider-Man wall-running upward as Otto throws debris and tentacles from below. Stage 3 returns to the rooftop with Otto fully unleashed, all four tentacles attacking simultaneously, and the campaign's hardest combo strings forcing perfect-dodge sequences and gadget management.

The chapter's emotional payoff is Otto's defeat. Stripped of his tentacle harness, broken on the rooftop, the man who taught Peter is finally just a man — and a man Peter loves. Otto's last words to Peter, staged in a quiet exchange before the police arrive, complete the campaign's most heartbreaking arc.

chapters.name: 13. The Sacrifice
chapters.summary:
The campaign's epilogue and its emotional climax. Spider-Man returns to the hospital with the recovered antiserum, only to discover that there is exactly one dose left — and that dozens of patients, including Aunt May, are dying citywide. Aunt May, fully aware of the math, chooses to give the dose to the city rather than to herself. The chapter is structured as a single extended dialogue between Peter and May at her bedside.

The conversation reveals that **May has always known Peter is Spider-Man**. She tells him she has been proud of him. She gives him permission to let her go. The scene is staged with no music, in long takes, with both performances — Yuri Lowenthal's Peter and Nancy Linari's May — landing among the most acclaimed in any PlayStation game.

The chapter closes with the campaign's epilogue. Peter and Mary Jane reconcile on a Manhattan rooftop. The city begins to recover. And in Peter's apartment, in the final scene before the credits, **Miles Morales** reveals that he, too, was bitten — and that he, too, now has powers. The line *"I think it's time we had a talk"* sets up the next decade of the franchise.

title: Star Wars Jedi: Fallen Order
guide: Star Wars Jedi: Fallen Order is Respawn Entertainment's 2019 Soulslike action-adventure set in the dark times after Order 66. Padawan survivor Cal Kestis attempts to rebuild the Jedi Order while hunted by Imperial Inquisitors. The walkthrough captures the full main campaign across two parts on PS5 Pro at 4K HDR 60FPS with PSSR upscaling, no commentary. The cinematic playthrough emphasizes the Metroidvania-style world design.

chapters.name: 1. Bracca — Scrapyard Apprentice
chapters.summary:
The campaign opens with **Cal Kestis**, a survivor of Order 66, working anonymously as a scrap-cutter on the Star Destroyer scrapyard of **Bracca** five years after the Empire's massacre of the Jedi. The opening sequence is structured as a slow-burn tonal piece: Cal lives in the workers' shanty town, hides his Force sensitivity, and works alongside his friend **Prauf**, an Abednedo scrapper. The chapter dwells on the texture of a post-Jedi galaxy — the visible Imperial oppression, the rationing, and the careful concealment Cal has constructed to survive.

The chapter's pivot is a workplace accident that forces Cal to instinctively use the Force to save a fellow worker. The act is witnessed by Imperial agents, and the resulting investigation brings the **Second Sister** — a Jedi-trained Imperial Inquisitor — to Bracca personally. Prauf is murdered. Cal is chased across the scrapyard in the campaign's first sustained combat tutorial.

The chapter ends with Cal extracted from Bracca by **Cere Junda**, a Jedi survivor turned anti-Imperial operative, and her pilot **Greez Dritus** aboard the *Mantis*. The campaign's central mission — to recover a Jedi holocron that contains the names of every Force-sensitive child the Order has ever identified — is introduced.

chapters.name: 2. Bogano — BD-1 and the Vault
chapters.summary:
The campaign's tutorial planet. **Bogano** is a small, lush moon where the late Jedi Master **Eno Cordova** hid the holocron Cere has been hunting. The chapter is structured as a sustained exploration of the moon's surface, with Cal acquiring the campaign's signature companion: **BD-1**, a small spherical droid Cordova left behind to guide a worthy Jedi to the vault.

The chapter introduces the campaign's traversal fundamentals — wall-running, double-jumping, vine-swinging, and the BD-1 healing and scanning systems that will define the campaign's puzzle and exploration loop. The combat density is light; the chapter's focus is environmental learning.

The chapter ends with Cal locating Cordova's holocron vault — sealed with a puzzle Cordova specifically designed so that only a Jedi who has visited multiple Zeffo-civilization tombs can solve it. The campaign's next planets — Zeffo, Kashyyyk, and Dathomir — are introduced as Cordova's required pilgrimage sites.

chapters.name: 3. Zeffo — The First Tomb
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first sustained dungeon planet. **Zeffo** is the homeworld of the Zeffo civilization — an extinct precursor culture that practiced Force-based engineering centuries before the Jedi Order. The chapter is structured as a vertical exploration of Zeffo's surface and tomb complexes, with new traversal abilities (Force-push, slow-time effect) introduced through the Tombs themselves.

The chapter's combat highlights include the campaign's first sustained encounter with **Imperial Purge Troopers** — anti-Jedi specialists equipped with electrostaffs and shield-piercing weapons. The Purge Troopers force Cal to integrate parry mechanics and Force-redirect attacks into his combat rotation.

The chapter ends with Cal acquiring the first Zeffo tomb's puzzle piece and the campaign's exploration loop now fully open. The next destination — **Kashyyyk** — is unlocked.

chapters.name: 4. Kashyyyk — The Wookiee Resistance
chapters.summary:
The campaign's mid-act political chapter. Cal arrives on the Wookiee homeworld **Kashyyyk** during the Imperial occupation, where **Saw Gerrera**'s rebel cell is conducting a sustained insurgency against the Empire's slave-labor camps. The chapter is structured as a multi-stage rebellion assistance arc, with combat ranging from Wookiee village skirmishes to a multi-stage AT-AT walker assault on an Imperial refinery.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's political stakes. Saw Gerrera — a recurring figure across the broader *Star Wars* franchise — is rendered as a paranoid but effective extremist whose methods Cal finds alarming. The Imperial occupation of Kashyyyk is rendered with significantly more environmental detail than the franchise has typically afforded the Wookiee homeworld.

The chapter ends with the Imperial refinery destroyed, **Tarfful** (the Wookiee chieftain) freed, and the second Zeffo puzzle piece recovered from the planet's interior.

chapters.name: 5. Zeffo (Return) — Trilla's Pursuit
chapters.summary:
A return to **Zeffo** for the second tomb, with the campaign's mid-act pursuit dynamic now fully active. The **Second Sister** — revealed in audio logs as **Trilla Suduri**, a former Jedi Padawan turned Inquisitor — has been tracking Cal across the campaign's planets and now confronts him at the second Zeffo tomb. The chapter is structured as a sustained dungeon-and-duel sequence.

The duel with the Second Sister is the campaign's first sustained Inquisitor confrontation. Trilla's combat style is the campaign's most demanding to date — dual-blade lightsaber stance, Force-redirected projectile attacks, and a multi-phase fight that demands mastery of the campaign's parry mechanics. The duel ends with Cal's narrow escape, Trilla's identity as a former Jedi exposed, and the campaign's central moral question — what the Empire has done to its former Jedi — fully introduced.

The chapter ends with Cal's recovery on the Mantis, Cere visibly disturbed by Trilla's identity, and the campaign's mid-third-act setup for the next planet.

chapters.name: 6. Dathomir — The Nightsisters
chapters.summary:
The campaign's darkest sustained chapter. **Dathomir** is the homeworld of the **Nightsisters**, a witch-coven of dark-side Force users whose order was nearly extinguished during the Clone Wars. Cal arrives in pursuit of the third Zeffo puzzle piece and immediately meets **Merrin** — the last surviving Nightsister, a teenage survivor who has spent the years since her order's destruction alone among the planet's ruins.

The chapter is structured as a sustained negotiation arc. Merrin is hostile to Cal — the Jedi were complicit in the Nightsisters' fall — and the chapter requires Cal to navigate her grief through both combat and dialogue. The combat highlights include encounters with **Undead Nightbrother warriors** that Merrin's magic resurrects to test Cal's resolve.

The chapter dwells on the campaign's most thematically dense conversations — Merrin's grief, Cal's PTSD from Order 66, and Cere's own complicated history with the dark side. The chapter ends with Merrin granting Cal access to the third Zeffo puzzle piece, and Merrin herself joining the *Mantis* crew permanently as the campaign's most acclaimed companion.

chapters.name: 7. Ilum — The Kyber Crystal Pilgrimage
chapters.summary:
The franchise's most cinematic single-planet chapter. **Ilum** — the ancient Jedi pilgrimage world where Padawans crafted their first lightsabers — has been hollowed out by the Empire and converted into a weapons-research facility. Cal arrives in pursuit of the second Zeffo tomb's central key, but the chapter pivots into the campaign's most emotionally complete sequence: Cal's pilgrimage to find a new kyber crystal of his own and to complete the lightsaber his master Jaro Tapal began.

The chapter is structured as a single sustained walk through the Imperial-occupied ice caverns of Ilum, with Cal's PTSD from Order 66 surfacing through Force-vision flashbacks. The combat density is light; the chapter's central focus is the construction of Cal's new lightsaber and his emotional reconciliation with his master's death.

The chapter ends with Cal's completed double-bladed lightsaber, his Force sensitivity stabilized, and the campaign's final destination — the Inquisitorius fortress on **Nur** — staged.

chapters.name: 8. The Subterrel Crypt — Final Holocron
chapters.summary:
A medium-length chapter that returns Cal to the third and final Zeffo tomb, with Trilla's reinforced Inquisitor team now waiting at the chamber's entrance. The chapter weaves the campaign's hardest puzzles with sustained combat across the tomb's deepest level, culminating in a final dueling sequence against the Second Sister.

The chapter's duel is the campaign's emotional centerpiece. Trilla is defeated, captured, and brought aboard the *Mantis*. The chapter's resolution stages the campaign's most acclaimed conversation — Cere confronting Trilla as her former Padawan, with the campaign's full backstory of betrayal and survival now finally articulated. Cal recovers the holocron with the list of every Force-sensitive child the Jedi Order knew of.

The chapter ends with Cal in possession of the holocron, Trilla murdered by **Darth Vader** in the *Mantis* itself, and the campaign's final pursuit now committed.

chapters.name: 9. Nur — Confrontation with Vader
chapters.summary:
The campaign's climactic chapter. **Nur** — the Inquisitorius fortress moon — is where Cal has decided to confront **Darth Vader** personally to recover Cere from his pursuit. The chapter is structured as a sustained infiltration of the Inquisitorius base, with combat against Purge Troopers, Stormtroopers, and the campaign's hardest sustained enemy encounters.

The chapter's climactic encounter is the **duel with Vader** himself. The fight is mechanically distinct from every prior boss in the campaign — Vader cannot be defeated. The encounter is staged as a sustained survival sequence, with Cal forced to flee through the fortress's collapsing infrastructure while Vader pursues with overwhelming Force-based attacks. The sequence is one of the most acclaimed in modern *Star Wars* storytelling.

The chapter ends with Cal and Cere narrowly escaping Nur, Vader's pursuit halted, and the campaign's central moral question — whether to use the holocron's list to recruit Force-sensitive children into a new Jedi order — staged for the final scene.

chapters.name: 10. The Holocron Decision — Bogano Return
chapters.summary:
The campaign's epilogue. Cal returns to **Bogano** to face the final moral choice the campaign has been building toward: whether to use Eno Cordova's holocron to begin building a new Jedi order, with the catastrophic risk of the Empire identifying and hunting the children, or whether to destroy the holocron and protect them from ever being targeted.

The chapter is structured as a single sustained conversation between Cal, Cere, Merrin, and Greez aboard the *Mantis*. The decision is Cal's. The campaign's resolution stages either choice with substantial emotional weight, and the credits roll over the *Mantis* departing Bogano's atmosphere.

The chapter's post-credits scene establishes the campaign's sequel, with the surviving cast committed to a galaxy-spanning resistance arc that *Survivor* will pick up five years later.

title: Star Wars Jedi: Survivor
guide: Star Wars Jedi: Survivor is Respawn's sequel to Fallen Order, set five years after the original. Cal Kestis is now a battle-hardened Jedi fighting the Empire as it tightens its grip on the galaxy. New abilities include dual-stance lightsaber styles (Single, Double-Bladed, Dual Wield, Blaster, Crossguard). The walkthrough captures the full main campaign across two parts on PS5 Pro at 4K HDR 60FPS with PSSR upscaling, no commentary.

chapters.name: 1. The Coruscant Infiltration
chapters.summary:
The campaign opens five years after the events of *Fallen Order*, with **Cal Kestis** infiltrating the upper floors of Coruscant in pursuit of **Senator Sejan**, a corrupt Imperial collaborator. The opening hour is structured as a sustained vertical infiltration of the Coruscanti skyline — Cal's traversal repertoire is fully expanded with the **dual-blade**, **double-bladed**, and reworked **crossguard** lightsaber stances introduced in the campaign's tutorial sequence.

The chapter's central event is the **Coruscant crash**. Cal's stolen Imperial shuttle is attacked by Imperial Inquisitor forces during the escape, with Cal's allies **Greez Dritus**, **Nightsister Merrin**, and **Cere Junda** scattered across the galaxy when their ship goes down. The chapter ends with Cal alone in the crashed Mantis, deep in the frontier moon of **Koboh**, with no operational support network and a galactic-scale Imperial bounty on his head.

chapters.name: 2. Koboh — Greez's Cantina
chapters.summary:
The campaign's central hub introduction. Cal awakens on **Koboh** in the care of **Greez Dritus**, the four-armed Mantis pilot from *Fallen Order*, who has spent the years between games operating a cantina in the frontier settlement of **Rambler's Reach**. The chapter is structured as a sustained exploration of Koboh's open-world territories, with Greez's cantina serving as the campaign's primary hub for the rest of the game.

The chapter's combat highlights include the campaign's first sustained encounter with **Bedlam Raiders** — a faction of mercenary scavengers led by the Imperial-aligned warlord **Rayvis**, whose army has been excavating Koboh's surface for High Republic-era technology. The Raiders introduce the campaign's most varied enemy class structure.

The chapter ends with Cal reuniting with **Bode Akuna**, a mercenary contact and family man who becomes the campaign's primary companion across its entire runtime.

chapters.name: 3. Jedha — The Hidden Path
chapters.summary:
A mid-act chapter that takes Cal off Koboh and onto **Jedha**, the desert pilgrimage moon glimpsed in *Rogue One*. The chapter opens with Cal arriving at the **Crypt of Uhrma** — a hidden Jedi sanctuary that **Cere Junda** has spent the years between games turning into a refugee center for force-sensitives and former Jedi. Cere's introduction is the campaign's first emotional anchor.

The chapter's central reveal is **Tanalorr** — a Jedi sanctuary world hidden in the Abyss between systems, accessible only via High Republic compass technology. The Hidden Path's purpose is to evacuate force-sensitives to Tanalorr before the Empire's purge reaches them. The chapter's exploration loop opens Jedha's pilgrim districts, the ruins of the former Imperial outpost, and the High Republic-era Crypt of Uhrma's deeper chambers.

The chapter ends with Cal in possession of the High Republic compass fragment, the campaign's central mission established, and the Hidden Path's broader network introduced.

chapters.name: 4. The Mantis Hangar — Bode's Family
chapters.summary:
A character-focused chapter that establishes the campaign's emotional core. **Bode Akuna**'s daughter **Kata** is introduced through Cal's visit to the family's hidden domestic life — a quiet bunker beneath Koboh's surface. The chapter dwells on Bode's relationship with his daughter, his deceased wife's connection to Cere's order, and the family's reasons for choosing to escape the Empire entirely rather than fight it.

The chapter's tone is deliberately gentler than the campaign's surrounding action sequences. The combat density drops; the dialogue depth increases. The chapter establishes the audience's investment in Bode's character before the campaign's third-act reversal will demand it.

The chapter ends with Cal committed to helping Bode and Kata reach Tanalorr alongside the rest of the Hidden Path.

chapters.name: 5. The Shattered Moon — High Republic Excavation
chapters.summary:
A mid-game vertical-traversal chapter set on the **Shattered Moon**, a remnant of a destroyed planet in the Koboh system. The chapter is structured as a sustained climbing-and-platforming sequence across the moon's broken surfaces, with new traversal abilities introduced — air-dashing, wall-clinging, and the **BD-1 grappling line** extension.

The chapter's combat highlights include the **Forest Array** sequence — a vertical Imperial transmitter complex that Cal traverses from base to summit while engaging Imperial troopers, droids, and the campaign's first sustained encounter with **Raider Lieutenants**. The traversal density and combat intensity escalate the campaign's mid-section significantly.

The chapter ends with Cal recovering a fragment of the High Republic excavation records, the existence of Tanalorr's defenses confirmed, and the campaign's mid-act open expansion of the Koboh open world.

chapters.name: 6. The Crypt of Uhrma — Dagan Gera
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first sustained antagonist introduction. **Dagan Gera**, a High Republic-era Jedi locked in bacta stasis beneath the Crypt of Uhrma since the fall of the Republic, is revived during Cal's exploration of the deep sanctuary. Dagan's introduction is initially staged as a possible ally — a Jedi master from the era of the High Republic, fluent in Force techniques the Empire's purge erased.

The chapter dwells on Dagan's slow revelation. The High Republic was not the idealistic golden age Cal has been taught; Dagan's order had its own crimes, its own betrayals, and Dagan himself was condemned by the Council for breaking the Jedi code. His freedom is the chapter's gift to him; his rage at his own order is the campaign's mid-act antagonist.

The chapter ends with Dagan revealed as the campaign's secondary antagonist, declaring his intention to seize Tanalorr for himself and his Raider army, and committing to defeating Cal across the rest of the campaign.

chapters.name: 7. Nova Garon — Imperial Fortress
chapters.summary:
The campaign's most demanding combat chapter. **Nova Garon** is an Imperial fortress moon serving as the headquarters of the Imperial Inquisitor's pursuit of Cal. The chapter is structured as a sustained infiltration of the fortress, with multiple Inquisitor confrontations, Stormtrooper platoons, and the campaign's first sustained AT-ST and TIE pilot encounters.

The chapter's central boss confrontation is the duel with **Inquisitor Drya Thornir** in the fortress's central command chamber. Thornir's combat style is the most demanding the campaign has staged, with multiple lightsaber stance switches and Force-based environmental attacks demanding mastery of the campaign's full repertoire.

The chapter ends with Thornir defeated, the Imperial pursuit of Cal significantly disrupted, and the campaign's mid-third-act assault on Tanalorr now positioning itself.

chapters.name: 8. The Mountain Ascent — Bode and Cal
chapters.summary:
A character-focused mid-third-act chapter staged across **Koboh's central mountain range**. Cal and Bode travel together on what the campaign frames as a routine reconnaissance mission, with the chapter dwelling in their friendship — Bode's stories about Kata's childhood, Cal's reflections on his master Cere, and the slow buildup of the campaign's central trust before its central betrayal.

The chapter's exploration loop opens the campaign's hardest puzzle dungeon — the **Lucrehulk wreckage** in the mountain's caldera, a Trade Federation-era warship that crashed during the Clone Wars and has been a Bedlam Raider stronghold ever since. The dungeon weaves traversal-based puzzles with the campaign's deepest single combat encounter.

The chapter ends with the wreckage cleared, the Tanalorr compass fully recovered, and the campaign's final reveal positioned for the next chapter.

chapters.name: 9. The Bode Betrayal
chapters.summary:
The campaign's central twist. **Bode Akuna** is revealed to be an Imperial deep-cover agent, planted in the Hidden Path under false pretenses with explicit instructions to recover the Tanalorr compass for Imperial use. The chapter is structured as a single sustained sequence: the revelation, the duel, Cere's death at Bode's hand, and Cal's emotional collapse.

The Bode-versus-Cal duel is the campaign's most acclaimed single confrontation. Bode is a Force-sensitive — kept hidden from Cal across the entire campaign — and his combat ability matches or exceeds Cal's. The duel ends with Bode escaping in the Mantis, Cere dying in the Crypt of Uhrma at Cal's side, and Tanalorr's location now in Imperial hands.

The chapter ends with the campaign's central question fully reframed: not whether Cal can save the Hidden Path, but whether he can become the man capable of stopping Bode before the Empire reaches Tanalorr.

chapters.name: 10. The Abyss — The Run to Tanalorr
chapters.summary:
The campaign's climactic act. Cal, **Merrin** (returned to active partnership), and the surviving Hidden Path forces stage a desperate run to Tanalorr through the **Abyss** — a hyperspace anomaly that destroys any ship not equipped with the High Republic compass. The chapter is structured as a sustained pursuit through hyperspace, with multiple Imperial intercepts and the campaign's most spectacle-heavy aerial sequences.

The chapter's combat highlights include the campaign's first sustained spaceship combat sequence, with Cal piloting a stripped-down Mantis through the Abyss's gravitational anomalies. The chapter's central tension is whether Bode's Imperial fleet or Cal's Hidden Path will reach Tanalorr first.

The chapter ends with Cal arriving at Tanalorr seconds ahead of the Empire, the Hidden Path's refugees secured on the surface, and Bode arriving alone on the planet for the campaign's climactic duel.

chapters.name: 11. Tanalorr — The Final Duel
chapters.summary:
The campaign's climactic chapter. **Tanalorr** is rendered as the campaign's most visually distinct location — a verdant Jedi sanctuary world untouched by the Empire's purge, with ancient High Republic architecture integrated into a thriving biosphere. The chapter is structured as a sustained pursuit-and-duel across the planet's central temple complex.

The final duel with **Bode Akuna** is one of the franchise's most acclaimed boss fights. Bode's combat style mirrors Cal's — both trained in similar Jedi traditions, both Force-sensitive, both emotionally invested in their separate causes — with the duel's emotional weight equal to its mechanical demands. The fight ends with Bode defeated and Cal forced to make a choice the campaign has been building toward: kill Bode, or attempt redemption.

The chapter ends with the player's chosen outcome played out in full, **Kata Akuna** processing the death of her father in front of Cal, and the Hidden Path's refugees beginning their new lives on Tanalorr.

chapters.name: 12. Epilogue — The Mantis Crew
chapters.summary:
The campaign's epilogue is staged on Tanalorr in the days following the final duel. The surviving cast — Cal, Merrin, Greez, Kata, and the Hidden Path's leadership — settle into the planet's new reality. The chapter dwells on the cost of the campaign on each character, with quiet conversations between Cal and Merrin about the future of the Jedi tradition, between Cal and Kata about Bode's legacy, and between Cal and Greez about what comes next.

The chapter's final scene establishes the franchise's third installment, with Cal departing Tanalorr on a mission the campaign deliberately leaves unspecified. The credits roll over the Mantis's departure into hyperspace. The campaign's central question — whether a Jedi can survive in an Empire that has spent two decades hunting his order — is answered, at least for now, in the affirmative.

title: Stellar Blade
guide: Stellar Blade is Shift Up's action title following EVE, a member of the 7th Airborne Squad sent from a space colony to reclaim Earth from the Naytibas, mutated creatures who drove humanity off the planet. The fast, precision-based combat draws comparison to Sekiro and NieR, with parries, dodges, and Beta/Burst skill trees. The walkthrough captures the full main campaign across three parts on PS5 Pro at 4K HDR 60FPS with PSSR upscaling, no commentary.

chapters.name: 1. Eidos 7 — The 7th Airborne Squad
chapters.summary:
The campaign opens with **EVE** — a member of the **7th Airborne Squad**, an elite force of Angels descended from the orbital **Mother Sphere** — leading a mass airborne assault on **Eidos 7**, one of Earth's last surviving cities. The opening sequence is structured as a sustained spectacle set-piece: dozens of Angels falling from orbit, the city's surface combat erupting on contact, and EVE's first sustained combat encounters against the **Naytibas** — the biological monstrosities that have overrun Earth.

The opening dwells on the campaign's central premise. Humanity, driven from Earth's surface generations ago by the Naytiba invasion, has retreated to the Mother Sphere orbital habitat. The 7th Airborne Squad has been dispatched to reclaim Earth one city at a time, with EVE's mission specifically targeting an **Alpha Naytiba** — a high-tier specimen whose elimination would significantly weaken the regional Naytiba ecosystem.

The chapter's pivot is the **annihilation of the 7th Airborne**. Within the first hour, the entire squad is slaughtered by the Alpha Naytiba and its accompanying forces, with EVE's mentor **Tachy** dying in EVE's arms during the chapter's mid-section. EVE survives only through the intervention of **Adam**, a Xion scavenger who pulls her unconscious body to safety. The chapter ends with EVE awakening in Adam's care, the campaign's central mission (eliminating the Alpha Naytiba) reaffirmed, and the path through Eidos 7's ruins committed.

chapters.name: 2. The Wasteland — Approaching Xion
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first sustained open-zone chapter. EVE and Adam traverse the **Wasteland** — the irradiated post-collapse landscape between Eidos 7 and the surviving human city of **Xion**. The chapter is structured as a sustained exploration sequence with the franchise's signature combat fundamentals fully introduced: EVE's dual-blade combat, the parry-and-counter system, the **Burst** energy-meter mechanic, and the campaign's most demanding sustained Naytiba combat encounters.

The chapter weaves combat with sustained character-development sequences. Adam, initially rendered as a self-interested scavenger, gradually emerges as the campaign's most morally complex supporting character — a former Xion citizen with significantly more historical knowledge of the Mother Sphere's operations than he initially admits. The chapter dwells on Adam's quiet skepticism toward EVE's mission directives.

The chapter ends with EVE and Adam's arrival at Xion's outer gates, the campaign's first sustained social environment opened, and the path to the survivor prophet **Orcal** committed.

chapters.name: 3. Xion — The Last Human City
chapters.summary:
The campaign's central hub chapter. **Xion** is rendered as the most architecturally distinctive single environment in the campaign — a vertical multi-level survivor settlement built into and around the ruins of a pre-collapse skyscraper complex, protected from Naytiba incursion by a magnetic field powered by a "personal link" maintained by Orcal himself.

The chapter is structured as a sustained social-exploration sequence with no combat. EVE meets the city's central inhabitants — **Roxanne**, the information broker; **Kaya**, the resource trader; **Mann**, the city's Sentinel security chief; and **Orcal**, the prophet whose personal link is the foundation of Xion's continued survival. The chapter dwells on Xion's slow recognition of EVE's Angel status — most citizens treat her with vocal distrust, with the Mother Sphere's intervention generations ago having actively failed to protect the surviving humans on Earth.

The chapter introduces **Lily**, a survivor of an earlier airborne operation from EVE's predecessor mission to Eidos 9. Lily's reunion with EVE is staged with deliberate emotional weight, with the chapter's mid-section establishing the partnership that will anchor the campaign's middle act.

The chapter ends with Orcal's first scan-mission committed, the path back into the Wasteland opened, and the campaign's central side-quest economy introduced through Xion's bulletin board system.

chapters.name: 4. The Wasteland Expansion — Side Quests and Combat
chapters.summary:
The campaign's first sustained side-content-integrated chapter. Returning to the **Wasteland** with Orcal's intelligence on additional Naytiba threats, EVE undertakes a series of sub-missions — recovering pre-collapse data archives, hunting down rogue Naytiba variants threatening Xion's supply caravans, and gathering resources required for Orcal's expanded scanning operation.

The chapter weaves combat with sustained environmental investigation. The Wasteland's broader geography is rendered with significantly more visual detail than the campaign's opening Eidos 7 environments, with the chapter introducing several of the campaign's most iconic enemy variants — the **Barnacles** (durable melee threats), the **Cricket Slashers** (fast ambush units), and the campaign's first sustained encounter with a **Beta-tier Naytiba**.

The chapter ends with the propulsion control board recovered, the Tetrapod aerial vehicle restored to operational status, and the campaign's middle act fully committed.

chapters.name: 5. Eidos 9 — Lily's Past
chapters.summary:
The campaign's most emotionally weighted single chapter. **Eidos 9** is the city where Lily's predecessor airborne mission failed, with the chapter staged as a sustained exploration of the ruins where Lily's entire prior squad perished. The chapter is structured as a sustained character-focused sequence weaving combat against the city's remaining Naytiba forces with the franchise's most acclaimed environmental storytelling.

The chapter dwells on Lily's character. Her quiet recognition of the spots where individual squadmates died, her measured commentary on the broader Mother Sphere mission framework that sent the squad to its deaths, and her growing distrust of the Mother Sphere command structure all anchor the chapter's emotional weight. The chapter ends with a critical data-archive recovered, the broader Mother Sphere conspiracy seeded, and the path to the campaign's third-act spectacle setting opened.

chapters.name: 6. The Great Desert — Spire 4 and Altess Levoire
chapters.summary:
The campaign's largest sustained exploration zone. The **Great Desert** is rendered as the campaign's most expansive environment, with the Tetrapod aerial vehicle enabling sustained traversal across multiple sub-zones including **Spire 4** (a pre-collapse research facility) and **Altess Levoire** (a remote ruin containing critical energy resources).

The chapter is structured as a sustained multi-zone investigation sequence with the campaign's hardest sustained combat encounters. Each sub-zone provides distinct gameplay loops — Spire 4's interior weaves combat with sustained environmental puzzles, while Altess Levoire's exterior expanses stage the campaign's most acclaimed open-zone combat sequences.

The chapter's central event is **Orcal's energy-resource scan**. The personal link's expanded operational capability, powered by the resources EVE recovers across this chapter, allows the prophet to scan the entire planet for Naytiba activity for the first time in decades. The scan reveals the Alpha Naytiba's location — and significantly more troubling, the existence of **Elder Naytibas**, a higher tier of organic threat the Mother Sphere has not previously acknowledged.

The chapter ends with the Alpha Naytiba's location pinpointed, the campaign's third-act objective committed, and the path to **Matrix 11** opened.

chapters.name: 7. Matrix 11 — The Pre-Collapse Truth
chapters.summary:
The campaign's revelation chapter. **Matrix 11** is a deep underground pre-collapse research facility containing the original data archives explaining how the Naytibas were created. The chapter is structured as a sustained interior exploration with significantly reduced combat density, dedicated entirely to the campaign's central narrative reveal.

The chapter's central reveal is the **origin of the Naytibas**. The pre-collapse research staff, working under early Mother Sphere directives, engineered the Naytiba biology as a biological weapons program. The program escaped containment, the Naytibas evolved beyond their original design parameters, and the resulting catastrophe is the foundation of the entire campaign's present-day political situation. The Mother Sphere has spent generations actively concealing its responsibility for the apocalypse it now positions itself as the solution to.

The chapter ends with EVE's understanding of her own role fundamentally reconfigured. Her mission — to eliminate the Alpha Naytiba — has been issued by the same Mother Sphere command structure that engineered the Naytiba threat in the first place. The campaign's central moral question — whether EVE should complete her mission or interrogate the directives that produced it — is fully active.

chapters.name: 8. The Final Confrontation — Alpha and Elder Naytiba
chapters.summary:
The campaign's climactic combat chapter. EVE confronts the **Alpha Naytiba** in a sustained boss confrontation at the Naytiba's central nesting ground. The chapter is structured as a multi-phase boss fight with the campaign's most demanding sustained combat sequences, weaving the Alpha's signature attack patterns with environmental hazards specific to the boss arena.

The chapter's mid-section pivots to the **Elder Naytiba** confrontation. The Elder is revealed as a significantly more powerful entity than the Alpha, with combat patterns drawing on the campaign's entire enemy roster across the runtime. The fight stages the campaign's mechanical apex, with EVE's full combat repertoire — every parry, every Burst skill, every Beta-tier ability — demanded for the encounter's resolution.

The chapter ends with the immediate Naytiba leadership eliminated, but the campaign's broader political crisis fully escalated. The Mother Sphere's compromised legitimacy and the surviving human community's contested future are positioned for the campaign's final decision sequence.

chapters.name: 9. The Final Decision — Three Endings
chapters.summary:
The campaign's epilogue chapter is staged as a sustained decision sequence with three possible outcomes. EVE, having confronted both the Naytiba leadership and the Mother Sphere's foundational deception, must choose between the campaign's three possible endings: **the Default Ending** (following Mother Sphere directives), **the Doomsday Ending** (rejecting the Mother Sphere but failing to provide an alternative), and **the True Ending** (rejecting the Mother Sphere while committing to the surviving human community's long-term protection).

The chapter dwells on the campaign's central thematic question. EVE's identity as an Angel — a Mother Sphere construct rather than a born human — is reframed across the final dialogue exchanges. Her relationships with Adam, Lily, and Xion's surviving population have given her a moral foundation independent of the Mother Sphere's command structure, and the campaign's chosen ending depends on the player's accumulated choices across the campaign's runtime.

The credits roll over the player's chosen ending configuration, with the campaign's central question — whether engineered beings can transcend their original purposes — answered through the player's specific moral commitments across the campaign's runtime. The post-credits content (**NieR: Automata** and **Goddess of Victory: NIKKE** crossover DLCs) is explicitly seeded for the franchise's continuation.

title: The Last of Us Part I Remastered
guide: The Last of Us Part I Remastered is Naughty Dog's PS5 ground-up remake of the 2013 PlayStation classic, with rebuilt assets, modern lighting, and updated AI. Smuggler Joel escorts immune teenager Ellie across a fungus-infested post-pandemic America in search of the Fireflies' research lab. The walkthrough captures the full main campaign across two parts on PS5 Pro at 4K HDR 60FPS with PSSR upscaling, no commentary, presented as a continuous cinematic playthrough.

chapters.name: 1. Hometown
chapters.summary:
The campaign's prologue and one of the most influential opening hours in modern gaming. The chapter is staged in **Austin, Texas, in 2013**, on the night that a fungal pandemic — the Cordyceps Brain Infection — pushes the United States past the point of recovery. The player begins as **Sarah**, Joel's twelve-year-old daughter, exploring the family home as news reports of unrelated violence flicker on the TV. The pacing is deliberately domestic and ordinary, with Sarah's bedroom, her father's birthday gift to her, and the family routine establishing the emotional stakes the rest of the chapter will detonate.

The chapter's central sequence is the **escape from Austin**. Joel returns from work in the middle of the night, the family — Joel, Sarah, and Joel's brother **Tommy** — attempts to flee the city by truck, and the player experiences the collapse of American civil order from the passenger seat. The sequence weaves road accidents, military checkpoints, infected pursuit through residential neighborhoods, and a flipped truck in a moment that has become canonical.

The chapter's final scene is the death of Sarah. Shot by a panicked soldier under orders to neutralize anyone potentially infected, Sarah dies in Joel's arms on a darkened roadside. The scene is staged with no score, in long takes, and Troy Baker's performance — alongside Hana Hayes as Sarah — defines the entire campaign's emotional foundation. The chapter ends with a cut to black and the title card.

chapters.name: 2. The Quarantine Zone
chapters.summary:
The chapter jumps forward **twenty years**. Joel is now a forty-eight-year-old smuggler operating out of the **Boston Quarantine Zone**, one of the last surviving U.S. military-controlled survivor enclaves. The chapter's first hour is structured as a tour of post-collapse America: ration lines, public executions, military checkpoints, and the deeply unhealthy ecosystem of black-market trade that keeps the QZ functioning. Joel's partnership with **Tess**, a fellow smuggler and his closest human connection, is established as both professional and quietly romantic.

The chapter's central sequence is the **botched smuggling job** that brings Joel and Tess to the warehouse where **Marlene**, leader of the Fireflies resistance, presents them with their next contract: smuggle a fourteen-year-old girl named **Ellie** out of the QZ in exchange for a major cache of supplies. Ellie's introduction is staged with deliberate ambiguity — Joel does not yet know she is immune to the infection, and the negotiation that brings her into his care is purely transactional.

The chapter closes with the trio sneaking out of the QZ at night, attempting to clear the city's Federal Disaster Response perimeter. The escape goes badly. A military scanner detects Ellie's bite scars, the patrol opens fire, and Joel kills a soldier in self-defense. The trio escapes into the post-apocalyptic outskirts of Boston with no clear path back. The chapter ends with the campaign committed to a journey nobody yet understands the scope of.

chapters.name: 3. The Outskirts
chapters.summary:
The chapter dwells in the ruined outer boroughs of Boston, with Joel, Tess, and Ellie navigating the city's collapsed infrastructure toward a Firefly rendezvous point at the **State House**. The chapter introduces the campaign's combat fundamentals in full: stealth-first encounter design, the listen-mode echolocation system, the crafting bench, the molotov and shiv recipes, and the resource-scarcity logic that defines the campaign's tension.

The chapter's central reveal is **Ellie's immunity**. Tess, scratched during a clicker encounter in a museum sequence, confronts Ellie about her bite — and discovers that Ellie was bitten three weeks ago and has shown no symptoms. Tess realizes she will turn within hours, but Ellie will not. The realization reframes the entire smuggling job. Tess insists Joel honor the contract and deliver Ellie to the Fireflies. The implications — that Ellie's blood could be the key to a vaccine — are deliberately understated.

The chapter ends at the State House. Tess, now visibly turning, holds off a pursuing military patrol while Joel and Ellie escape through a back stairwell. Tess's death — staged in a sustained gunfight with Joel and Ellie watching from cover — is the campaign's first major loss after the prologue. The chapter ends with the two of them committed to a journey neither of them wants.

chapters.name: 4. Bill's Town
chapters.summary:
A character-focused chapter set in **Lincoln, Massachusetts**, the trap-laden domain of **Bill** — a paranoid, conspiracy-driven survivor who owes Joel a favor and has the resources to provide a working vehicle. The chapter is staged across Lincoln's overgrown streets, with Bill's elaborate booby-trap network catching infected, military patrols, and Joel himself in sequence. The opening combat tutorial weaves stealth, environmental kills, and the campaign's first major **clicker** encounters into the chapter's geography.

The chapter's central thread is Bill's quiet grief. Frank — Bill's partner, never named directly in the original cut, but present in his belongings and his suicide note — has died before Joel's arrival. The chapter's emotional centerpiece is the discovery of Frank's body in a safehouse attic, hanged, with a note explaining that he could not live with Bill's bunker-paranoia and the world it was preparing for. Bill reads the note in silence. The campaign's later expansions would revisit this story; the original cut leaves it intact and devastating.

The chapter closes with Bill providing a working car battery, Joel and Ellie driving out of Lincoln in a salvaged Chevy pickup, and the journey moving toward Pittsburgh.

chapters.name: 5. Pittsburgh
chapters.summary:
The campaign's most extended combat chapter. The driving sequence ends in an ambush — Joel and Ellie are run off the road by a Hunter blockade, and the rest of the chapter is structured around their escape on foot through the bombed-out downtown of Pittsburgh. The Hunters, a faction of post-collapse human survivors who prey on travelers, are introduced as the campaign's first sustained human enemy. The combat shifts from infected-focused stealth to human-on-human gunfights with significantly different mechanical rhythms.

The chapter's open-world feel — multiple parallel routes, optional building infiltrations, and resource-rich exploration paths — gives it the campaign's most pure "survival horror open level" design. The hotel basement sequence, in particular, is one of the campaign's most acclaimed set-pieces: a flooded service tunnel, a generator restart, and a clicker swarm in pitch darkness that introduces the campaign's hardest stealth encounter to date.

The chapter ends with Joel and Ellie meeting **Henry** and his younger brother **Sam**, fellow survivors of the Pittsburgh Hunters who are trying to reach a remote refuge. The four of them agree to travel together. The chapter closes with the group hidden in a Hunter-cleared apartment building, planning their escape across the Allegheny River.

chapters.name: 6. The Suburbs
chapters.summary:
A shorter chapter that dwells in the relative quiet of Pittsburgh's outer suburbs as the four travelers prepare to cross the river and head west. The combat density drops significantly; the chapter's focus is on the developing dynamic between Ellie and Sam, two teenagers from radically different post-collapse upbringings, and on Joel's growing parental discomfort with how attached Ellie is becoming to anyone.

The chapter's central event is the **death of Sam**. Bitten during the previous chapter's river crossing and concealed by Henry, Sam turns in the night and attacks Ellie. The campaign forces the player to watch Henry shoot his own brother before turning the weapon on himself. The scene is staged in a single sustained take, with no music, and lands as one of the campaign's most devastating losses.

The chapter closes with Joel and Ellie burying the brothers in the suburbs and continuing west alone. The road has narrowed to just the two of them again, and the campaign's central relationship has nowhere left to deflect into.

chapters.name: 7. Tommy's Dam
chapters.summary:
A reprieve chapter set at the **Jackson Hole hydroelectric dam**, where Joel's brother Tommy and his wife Maria have built a working post-collapse community. The chapter opens with Joel and Ellie arriving at the dam in the late afternoon, Tommy's reunion with Joel staged across the chapter's first half. The community is presented as the campaign's first genuinely functional post-collapse society, with farming, electricity, schools, and a working defensive perimeter.

The chapter's central conflict is **Joel's attempt to hand off Ellie**. Convinced he is not the right person to escort her the rest of the way, Joel asks Tommy to take her instead. Ellie overhears the conversation, runs from the dam, and the chapter's middle act is a horseback pursuit across the Wyoming wilderness to bring her back. The conversation that follows — Ellie confronting Joel about Sarah, about what he is afraid of, about why he keeps pushing her away — is one of the campaign's most pivotal scenes.

The chapter ends with Joel changing his mind. He will take Ellie the rest of the way himself. They depart Jackson the next morning, riding south toward the Fireflies' lab at the University of Eastern Colorado.

chapters.name: 8. The University
chapters.summary:
A medium-length chapter set in the abandoned campus of the **University of Eastern Colorado**, where the Fireflies are rumored to have established their primary research facility. The campus is rendered in the campaign's most autumnal lighting — fall foliage, low sun, empty quads — and the chapter's exploration loop weaves Joel and Ellie through dormitories, lecture halls, and a Fireflies-converted biology department.

The chapter's central reveal is that **the Fireflies have moved**. The University facility is abandoned, with audio logs and lab notes revealing that the research team relocated months ago to a hospital in Salt Lake City. The trip has been, in one sense, for nothing. The deeper realization — that the Fireflies are clearly conducting active research on the infection, with hints that the methodology is not benign — is left for the audience to put together.

The chapter ends with Joel and Ellie ambushed by a Hunter patrol in the campus library. The escape sequence is brutal: Joel is impaled on a piece of rebar during a fall from a second-floor balcony, the wound is severe, and Ellie is forced to drag him to relative safety while a winter storm closes in. The chapter cuts to black with Joel unconscious and Ellie alone.

chapters.name: 9. Lakeside Resort
chapters.summary:
The campaign's most acclaimed perspective-shift chapter. Joel is comatose for most of the chapter, recovering from his rebar wound in an abandoned lakeside vacation house, while **Ellie** — now the player character — hunts in the surrounding winter wilderness to keep them alive. The chapter is staged in heavy snow, with the player's vision limited and the cold pressing against the survival mechanics in ways the campaign has not previously demanded.

The chapter's central thread is **David**, a charismatic survivor leader who befriends Ellie during her hunting expeditions. David's group has been pursuing Joel and Ellie since Pittsburgh, and David's slow grooming of Ellie — staged with deliberate menace — reveals that his community is composed of cannibals who have been preying on travelers all winter. The chapter pivots from survival drama to horror in the second act.

The chapter ends with one of the most acclaimed extended sequences in the franchise: **Ellie's hatchet murder of David** in a burning restaurant kitchen, after his attempted assault. The scene is staged in flickering fire light, with no music, and lands as Ellie's irreversible loss of childhood. Joel — recovered enough to walk — arrives moments too late. The chapter closes with him carrying her, exhausted and traumatized, back into the woods.

chapters.name: 10. Bus Depot
chapters.summary:
A short transitional chapter set in the spring, with Joel and Ellie having traveled into the high country surrounding Salt Lake City. The chapter dwells in the quiet aftermath of the Lakeside Resort. Ellie is visibly closed off, Joel is visibly aware of it, and the two of them spend most of the chapter not speaking.

The chapter's emotional centerpiece is a giraffe sequence inside an abandoned bus depot. The wandering giraffe — a deliberate visual contradiction to the post-apocalyptic world the campaign has built — gives Ellie a single moment of genuine wonder, and Joel's quiet recognition that he is going to have to give her up to the Fireflies anchors the campaign's final emotional turn.

The chapter ends with the two of them sighting Salt Lake City in the distance and beginning their descent toward the Firefly hospital.

chapters.name: 11. The Firefly Lab
chapters.summary:
The campaign's climax. Joel and Ellie are intercepted at the river outside Salt Lake City by a Firefly patrol, knocked unconscious, and transported to **St. Mary's Hospital**, the Fireflies' research facility. The chapter opens with Joel recovering in a hospital bed under Marlene's supervision, learning that the surgical procedure required to extract a vaccine from Ellie's brain will kill her — and that the Fireflies have already prepped her for surgery.

The chapter pivots to one of the franchise's most morally loaded set-pieces. Joel — armed by a sympathetic Firefly nurse — fights his way through the hospital's surgical wing, killing dozens of Fireflies along the way, including unarmed surgeons and the surgeon currently in mid-procedure on Ellie. The combat is staged without the campaign's usual stealth options; the design forces direct, sustained violence in a way the campaign has resisted up to this point. The implication is intentional.

The chapter ends with Joel carrying Ellie out of the operating room, killing **Marlene** in the parking garage to prevent her pursuit, and lying to Ellie about what happened — telling her the Fireflies "found dozens like her" and that "there are no vaccines coming." The lie is staged in the truck cab, in close-up, with Joel watching Ellie's reaction. The chapter cuts to black on the highway out of Salt Lake.

chapters.name: 12. Jackson
chapters.summary:
The campaign's epilogue, set months later in the **Jackson Hole community**. Joel and Ellie have walked back to Tommy's settlement, and the chapter is staged as a quiet domestic afternoon — Tommy and Maria's home, the community's children, the working farm. The combat is gone. The journey is over.

The chapter's central scene is Ellie's confrontation with Joel on a Jackson hillside. She asks him directly whether he has been telling her the truth about the Fireflies. Joel repeats the lie, this time with more weight, and asks her to believe him. Ellie's response — *"Okay"* — is one of the most studied single-word performances in the medium. The campaign does not resolve the question of whether Ellie believes him or whether she is simply choosing to accept the lie in order to preserve what they have built.

The chapter ends with the two of them walking together back toward the Jackson gate. The credits roll over the campaign's signature score. *Part II* is years away, but the seed of its central conflict — Joel's lie and the cost of it — is already planted in the final frame.

title: The Last of Us Part II Remastered
guide: The Last of Us Part II Remastered is Naughty Dog's PS5 remaster of the 2020 sequel, adding the No Return roguelite mode, Lost Levels, and rebuilt rendering pipeline. The story follows Ellie pursuing revenge across post-apocalyptic Seattle, with the controversial mid-game switch to antagonist Abby's perspective. The walkthrough captures the full main campaign across three parts on PS5 Pro at 4K HDR 60FPS with PSSR upscaling, no commentary.

chapters.name: 1. The Farm — Jackson
chapters.summary:
The campaign opens in **Jackson, Wyoming**, four years after the events of *Part I*. Ellie, now nineteen, lives on a working ranch outside the town walls alongside her surrogate-uncle Joel and his brother Tommy. The opening sequence is built as a slow domestic immersion in a community that has spent four years stabilizing into something genuinely functional — schools, livestock, electricity, a militia, and a fragile peace with the surrounding wilderness. Ellie is no longer a child, has a girlfriend in Dina, has friends in Jesse and Jesse's pregnant ex Cat, and lives in a converted garage on Joel's property that the campaign dwells on with deliberate intimacy.

The chapter's central event is the **Patrol** — Joel and his patrol partner Tommy ride out on a routine sweep of the territory north of Jackson, intercepting walkers and clearing infected nests as part of the town's standard security rotation. The patrol is staged with no music, in falling snow, and the chapter pacing deliberately mirrors the calm of the opening domestic sequences.

The chapter ends with Joel and Tommy taking shelter from a sudden infected horde in an abandoned ski lodge on the edge of town. Inside, they find a wounded young woman named **Abby** and her group of WLF survivors. Joel offers help. Joel introduces himself by name. The chapter cuts to black.

chapters.name: 2. The Death of Joel
chapters.summary:
The campaign's pivot. Abby and her group — Owen, Manny, Mel, Nora, Jordan, and Leah — recognize Joel by name immediately. The rest of the chapter is staged as a sustained, unbroken sequence in the ski lodge basement, with Joel restrained, Tommy beaten, and Ellie — who has been tracking them through the storm — arriving moments too late. Abby executes Joel with a golf club, in front of Ellie, in a sequence the campaign refuses to soften.

The chapter's emotional staging refuses every convention the franchise's first installment built. There is no last conversation, no thematic closure, no music cue. The chapter ends with Tommy and Ellie left alive in the lodge — alive specifically so they can carry the message back to whoever else loved Joel — and Abby's group disappearing into the storm.

The chapter cuts forward to Jackson in the days following. Joel's funeral. The town's reckoning. Ellie's decision, made over Maria's objection, to ride west to Seattle in pursuit of Abby's WLF faction. The campaign's central engine is now committed.

chapters.name: 3. The Pursuit Begins — Seattle Day 1 (Ellie)
chapters.summary:
Ellie and Dina arrive in **Seattle, Washington**, the post-collapse home of the **Washington Liberation Front (WLF)** — a paramilitary survivor faction that controls the city's central districts from a converted football stadium. The chapter is structured as an open-zone investigation through downtown Seattle, with the WLF's territorial markers, patrol patterns, and surveillance infrastructure rendered as one of the campaign's most dense urban environments.

The chapter introduces the **infected variations** that define *Part II*'s ecosystem — the Shamblers, the new Stalkers, and the Bloater hunting parties that the original game never staged at this density. The combat encounters dwell in the slow stealth fundamentals the franchise has refined across two games, but the environments are larger, the patrol routes longer, and the cost of detection significantly higher.

The chapter's central thread is the methodical pursuit of Abby's group through the WLF's identification systems. Ellie and Dina hunt for names, for affiliations, for the operational base where Abby is believed to be stationed. The chapter dwells in the cold-procedural mechanics of revenge — every interrogation, every body, every choice — and the campaign refuses to let the player feel righteous about any of it.

chapters.name: 4. Hostile Territory — Hillcrest
chapters.summary:
A mid-Day-1 chapter set in the **Hillcrest** residential neighborhood north of downtown Seattle. The biome is rendered as an overgrown suburb, with single-family homes consumed by ivy, abandoned cars, and a WLF patrol presence that suggests Hillcrest is being actively contested. The chapter's exploration loop opens optional house infiltrations, scavenger encounters, and one of the campaign's most acclaimed quiet sequences — a music store where Ellie plays Joel's guitar.

The chapter's combat highlights include the campaign's first sustained **WLF dog handlers**. The dogs introduce a new sensory tracking mechanic — they follow scent trails on the ground, forcing the player to vary their pathing in ways the franchise's prior combat designs did not demand. The dogs also force the campaign's first genuinely uncomfortable choice: the player must kill an animal that has done nothing wrong, in order for Ellie to continue.

The chapter ends with Ellie and Dina recovering critical intelligence on Abby's location and the WLF's intelligence-gathering aquarium base.

chapters.name: 5. The Aquarium — Owen
chapters.summary:
The transition between Days 1 and 2, structured as an extended infiltration of the **Seattle Aquarium**, a derelict pre-collapse facility that the WLF has repurposed as a quiet intelligence-station for its leadership. The chapter is staged in low light, with bioluminescent water tanks providing most of the illumination, and the design weaves stealth fundamentals through tight, claustrophobic corridors.

The chapter's central confrontation is with **Owen Moore**, one of Abby's group from the lodge. Owen is the campaign's first sustained antagonist who is rendered as a recognizably decent person — he is visibly grieving, visibly conflicted about Joel's killing, and visibly in love with both Abby and his partner Mel. The chapter's encounter with Owen ends in his death at Ellie's hand, with the killing staged in extended close-up and the immediate, terrible discovery that **Mel was pregnant.**

The chapter ends with Ellie and Dina escaping the aquarium with the location of Abby's base, the campaign's first sustained moral question landing in full force.

chapters.name: 6. Seattle Day 2 (Ellie) — Tracking Nora
chapters.summary:
Ellie's second day in Seattle is the campaign's longest single combat day, structured as a methodical hunt through a series of WLF-controlled districts in pursuit of **Nora Harris**, the medical lieutenant who was at Joel's killing and one of Ellie's last remaining named targets. The chapter's exploration loop opens **Seattle Hospital**, one of the campaign's most acclaimed level designs — a multi-floor environment that mixes WLF patrols with the campaign's most extensive infected encounter.

The chapter's central encounter is the **basement chase**. Nora flees into the hospital's flooded lower floors, drawing Ellie through an infected nest the WLF has been actively avoiding. The chase ends with Nora exposed to spores, dying of the resulting fungal infection — and Ellie torturing the dying woman for information about Abby's location. The chapter refuses to make the torture sequence palatable. The campaign explicitly stages it as the moral point of no return.

The chapter ends with Ellie returning to the theater base she and Dina have made their forward operations point, the first **Nightmare** PTSD sequence forcing the player to experience what Ellie has been numbing herself through, and the campaign's central question — what does revenge cost — fully open.

chapters.name: 7. The Theater — Day 2 End
chapters.summary:
A relatively short chapter staged inside the **abandoned Capitol Theater** in downtown Seattle, where Ellie, Dina, Jesse (recently arrived from Jackson), and Tommy reunite for one night between the campaign's hunting days. The chapter is structured as a series of quiet rooftop and backstage conversations: Ellie and Dina argue about the pregnancy; Ellie and Tommy argue about Abby; Jesse and Ellie reckon with how much of each of them remains.

The chapter dwells on what the campaign has been refusing to acknowledge: that Ellie's revenge mission has put Dina's pregnancy at lethal risk, that Tommy is operating on his own private version of the same revenge mission with significantly less restraint, and that Jesse is here because someone has to bring the survivors home. The campaign's first quiet, character-only chapter in a long stretch lands with substantial emotional weight.

The chapter ends with the group's decision to make one final raid the following day, with the goal of locating Abby herself and ending the mission once and for all.

chapters.name: 8. Ground Zero — Seattle Day 3 (Ellie)
chapters.summary:
The campaign's most demanding combat day. Ellie's third day in Seattle is structured around the assault on the **WLF stadium**, a fortified complex that serves as the WLF faction's military and civilian center. The chapter weaves sustained combat against military-grade WLF forces with the campaign's first prolonged engagement with the WLF's heaviest infected response teams.

The chapter's central confrontation is the **death of Jesse**. Returning from a side mission with Tommy, Jesse is shot through the head by a WLF sniper in a sequence staged with no warning, no buildup, and no music. The killing is one of the campaign's most acclaimed staging decisions — it removes a character the campaign has carefully built, in a moment where the player has been trained to expect tension rather than tragedy.

The chapter ends with Ellie's first direct confrontation with Abby herself in the theater basement. The fight is brutal, ends with Ellie about to deliver a killing blow, and is interrupted by the campaign's most controversial structural decision — **the perspective shifts.**

chapters.name: 9. Seattle Day 1 (Abby) — The Stadium
chapters.summary:
The campaign's perspective inversion. The same three days the player has just spent hunting Abby are now staged again, from her side, beginning with her arrival back at the WLF stadium the morning after Joel's killing in the ski lodge. The campaign asks the player to spend the next ten hours playing the woman who killed Joel, and to do so with the same camera angles, the same combat depth, and the same emotional intimacy the original days afforded Ellie.

The chapter opens with Abby's domestic life at the stadium — her relationships with Owen, Mel, Manny, Jordan, and the elderly WLF medic **Isaac Dixon**. The stadium is rendered as a functional community with significant moral texture: schools, civilian neighborhoods, mess halls, and the obvious investment of years of effort into making a place worth defending. The campaign forces the player to recognize that the people Ellie has been killing across the previous chapters had names, families, and unconditional belonging to one another.

The chapter ends with Abby leaving the stadium on a routine mission and discovering that her WLF patrol has been ambushed by the Seraphites — the rival religious faction the campaign has only glimpsed in the Ellie chapters.

chapters.name: 10. The Forest — Yara and Lev
chapters.summary:
Abby's mid-game arc and the campaign's most thematically reframing chapter. While conducting a sniper rescue operation on the WLF's eastern frontier, Abby intervenes in the public execution of two **Seraphite** children — **Yara**, a teenage girl, and **Lev**, her younger brother, who has been condemned for cutting his own hair and rejecting the Seraphite faith's gender norms. Abby's rescue is staged with no calculation: she does it because the killings are wrong. The chapter pivots Abby from antagonist to protagonist in a single sustained sequence.

The chapter's combat highlights include the campaign's first sustained Seraphite engagements. The Seraphites are mechanically distinct from the WLF — silent communication via whistles, bow-and-arrow primary armament, and a religious commitment to the cause that the chapter renders without satire. The chapter is the franchise's most empathetic portrait of an antagonist faction.

The chapter ends with Yara critically wounded by a Seraphite arrow, Lev permanently bonded to Abby, and Abby's WLF loyalties beginning to fracture as her commanders demand the children's surrender.

chapters.name: 11. The Coast — Seraphite Island
chapters.summary:
Abby's Seattle Day 3, staged as a sustained infiltration of the **Seraphite island stronghold** off the Seattle coast. The chapter's exploration loop is the campaign's most architecturally ambitious — a religious commune built into the island's cliff face, with shrine complexes, cult-staged civilian housing, and the campaign's most distinctive Seraphite combat encounters.

The chapter's emotional centerpiece is Yara's amputation surgery. Performed by Abby in a candlelit warehouse with Lev watching, the sequence stages the chapter's central thematic argument: that grace under impossible circumstance is what humanity actually consists of, and that the campaign's antagonist has been carrying as much of it as its protagonist. The chapter ends with Yara walking again, the Seraphite leader **Emily** publicly turning on Lev, and Abby committing to leading her new family out of Seattle entirely.

The chapter closes with the WLF's massed assault on the Seraphite island — Isaac Dixon's long-planned final solution — and Abby caught between her faction's military structure and the family she has assembled. Her decision to defect from the WLF closes the campaign's second day.

chapters.name: 12. The Confrontation — Seattle Day 3 (Abby)
chapters.summary:
The campaign's structural climax. Abby, Yara, and Lev attempt to escape Seattle entirely, only to find the WLF has burned every passage out of the city. The chapter is structured as a desperate retreat through the **Hillcrest** neighborhood — the same biome Ellie hunted through on her first day in the city — with the perspective inverted: the player now flees the very ambushes Ellie set.

The chapter's emotional crescendo is the **death of Yara**. Cut down by an Isaac Dixon sniper team in the same alley Jesse was shot in earlier in the campaign, Yara dies in Abby's arms as the camera holds on Lev's grief. The chapter is one of the campaign's most studied character beats — it reframes the campaign's central question of grief one final time.

The chapter ends with Abby's return to the theater for a final confrontation: the same fight the campaign cut away from in Ellie's Day 3. This time the player is on the other side of it.
